SERVICE AGREEMENT FOR THE PROVISION OF ELECTION SERVICES  
BETWEEN THE CITY OF                AND  
SAN MATEO COUNTY CHIEF ELECTIONS OFFICER & ASSESSOR-COUNTY CLERK-RECORDER 
 
 
This Service Agreement for the Provision of Election Services (“Agreement”), entered into this _______ day of 
_______________, 2024, by and between the City of               (the “Municipality”) and San Mateo County Chief 
Elections Officer & Assessor – County Clerk – Recorder (the “Chief Elections Officer”); 
 
WHEREAS, it is necessary and desirable that the Chief Elections Officer be retained for the purpose of conducting an 
election, described in more detail below, for the Municipality; and 
 
WHEREAS, the Chief Elections Officer has been asked by the Municipality to conduct an election on            , 2024 
(the “Election”). 
 
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS HEREBY AGREED BY THE PARTIES HERETO AS FOLLOWS: 
 
SERVICES TO BE PERFORMED BY THE MUNICIPALITY: 
 
1) 
Within the time frame set by statute, the Municipality will request the Board of Supervisors, through the Chief 
Elections Officer, to conduct an election relating to the Municipality on               , 2024 and will request the 
services of the Chief Elections Officer in relation to the Election.  
 
2) 
The Municipality will publish the Notice of Election and the Notice to File Declarations of Candidacy for the 
offices to be voted on and/or the Notice to File Arguments For or Against any measure. 
 
3) 
The Municipality will submit to the Chief Elections Officer the offices to be voted on and the measure ballot 
questions by 88 days prior to the Election. The Municipality will submit to the Chief Elections Officer the 
names and ballot destinations of the candidates to appear upon the ballot by 81 days prior to the Election. 
 
4) 
The Municipality will prepare and deliver to the Chief Elections Officer the ballot pamphlet information 
containing, as applicable, candidate designations and statements, ballot measure(s), tax rate statement(s), 
impartial analyses, arguments for or against measures and rebuttals thereto. 
 
5) 
The Municipality will review and sign off on the official ballot wording for measures. 
 
6) 
The Municipality will complete any other non-delegable tasks required by law in relation to the Election. 
 
7) 
The Municipality shall maintain records/maps regarding the boundaries of the Municipality and will notify the 
Chief Elections Officer of any changes/additions to those boundaries by 125 days prior to the Election. 
 
SERVICES TO BE PERFORMED BY CHIEF ELECTIONS OFFICER: 
 
1) 
The Chief Elections Officer will select the sample and official ballot printer(s) and translators. 
 
2) 
The Chief Elections Officer will prepare and deliver all election information to the printers and translators. 
 
3) 
The Chief Elections Officer will determine the appropriate translation and transliteration of all pertinent 
documents. 
 
4) 
The Chief Elections Officer will issue, receive and process vote by mail ballots. 
 
5) 
The Chief Elections Officer will set up ADA compliant Vote Centers, publish any required notices and conduct 
the Election. 
 
6) 
The Chief Elections Officer will provide services for any official recount or election contest, if applicable. 
 
7) 
The Chief Elections Officer will conduct all aspects of the Canvass of Votes Cast.  Pursuant to Section 10262 
of the Elections Code, the Chief Elections Officer will submit a Certificate of Chief Elections Officer to the 
Municipality’s governing body certifying the results of the Election. 
235 of 671

--- page 236 ---
8) 
The Chief Elections Officer will conduct other various and miscellaneous election-related activities directly 
required to conduct the Election itself.  To the extent that the Municipality has obligations under law to perform 
various duties that relate to the Election beyond those directly involved with conducting the Election, those 
duties remain the responsibility of the Municipality.  If the Municipality wishes to have any such duties 
performed by the Chief Elections Officer, the parties must mutually agree in advance in writing to have the 
Chief Elections Officer perform such duties.  By way of example only, if the Municipality is required to send 
certain notices or adopt resolutions relating to the Election, those duties remain duties of the Municipality. 
 
TERMS: 
 
This Agreement shall be in effect for the performance of all services incident to the preparation and conduct of the 
Election to be held on                , 2024, including before and after said Election date. 
 
In the event the Chief Elections Officer is unable to perform services required under this Agreement as a result of 
employer/employee relation conditions, vendor conditions, the unavailability of Federal, State, or County funds, 
COVID-19-related limitations, and/or other conditions beyond the control of the Chief Elections Officer, the Chief 
Elections Officer will be relieved of all obligations under this Agreement.  The Chief Elections Officer may terminate 
this Agreement after giving 72 hours written notice, at which time the Chief Elections Officer will be relieved of all 
obligations under this Agreement. 
 
This Agreement can be terminated by the Municipality upon 30 days written notice. 
 
COST FOR SERVICES 
 
In consideration of the performance of services and supplies provided by the Chief Elections Officer, including 1) any 
and all costs incurred during a recount or election contest that are not reimbursed by the voter requesting the recount 
or filing the contest as specified in the Elections Code and 2) any other costs associated with the Election, such as 
return postage costs and costs for ensuring voter accessibility, the Municipality shall pay to the Chief Elections Officer 
a sum equal to the full cost of the Election, including all such services, supplies, and other costs. 
 
The Chief Elections Officer shall send an itemized invoice to the Municipality for all services provided pursuant to this 
Agreement after the Election is conducted and all related costs are determined.  Payment on the full amount of the 
invoice shall be due and the Municipality shall submit payment to the County of San Mateo within forty-five (45) days 
of the date of the invoice (the “Due Date”).  If the amount is not paid in full within this time, interest shall accrue 
monthly at a rate of 0.25% per month (equivalent to 3% annually) on the unpaid balance starting at the Due Date.  
Thereafter, invoices will be sent and shall be payable within thirty (30) days of the date of the invoice, with interest 
being added each month for any unpaid balance.

SUBJECT:
Private Development and Right-of-Way Support Services – Agreements
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve professional consultant services agreements for wireless permit review support services through June 30, 2026, 
with up to two optional one-year extensions, with: Envirobusiness, Inc. dba EBI Consulting, in an amount not to exceed 
$100,000; OSC Engineering in an amount not to exceed $100,000; and Waterford Consultants, LLC in an amount not to 
exceed $100,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreements in substantially the form presented 
and issue amendments within the budget amount including no-cost term extensions.
BACKGROUND:
The Public Works Development Section is responsible for providing review and permitting for Wireless Permits within the 
public right-of-way. In 2021, Public Works updated San Mateo Municipal Code (SMMC) Chapter 17.10 "Wireless 
Communication Facilities in the Public Right-of-Way and the Design Standards” to apply to Wireless Permit applications 
involving new or existing wireless facilities within the public right-of-way. 
Wireless Permits:
Public Works received 178 wireless permit applications in 2023. As required by the Federal Communications Commission 
(FCC), City staff must review Wireless Permit applications within a 90 days (60 days for installations on existing structures) 
review timeframe also known as the "shot clock.” The shot clock starts once the applicant submits the application and 
stops when the permit is approved, which includes the appeal process and decision. If staff cannot complete the review 
within the shot clock, the application is automatically deemed approved by existing federal law.
FCC RF Emission Compliance:
Pursuant to 47 U.S.C 332(c)(7), the City may not regulate the placement, construction, and modification of wireless service 
facilities to the extent that such facilities comply with the FCC’s regulations concerning radio frequency (RF) emissions.  
Wireless Permit applications are required to include an RF compliance report prepared by an RF engineer to certify the 
proposed facility, as well as any collocated facilities and any cumulative emissions from adjacent areas, will comply with 
applicable FCC regulations. 
These reports are prepared and stamped by licensed Electrical Engineers; however, City staff typically does not have the 
technical expertise to verify the calculations and results of these reports. Over the past year, the City has received public 
input voicing a desire for the City to peer review and verify RF Report calculations and results using independent third-
party consultants.
Consultant Proposals:
237 of 671

--- page 238 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
In March 2024, Public Works issued a request for proposals (RFP) to multiple firms capable of providing RF Report review 
services on an on-call basis. In total, Public Works received responses from five (5) firms and evaluated those submissions 
based on the firms’ experience in RF Report preparation and/or review, the proposed firms’ staff qualifications, and the 
pricing structures. Ultimately, Public Works utilized those criteria to identify three (3) firms to consider awarding 
agreements for on-call RF Report review services. The firms selected by Public Works are:
•
Envirobusiness, Inc. (d/b/a EBI Consulting)
•
OSC Engineering
•
Waterford Consultants, LLC
Conflicts of Interest:
These three firms have or currently provide services to wireless service providers (such as AT&T, Crown Castle, T-Mobile, 
and Verizon). These three firms have confirmed that they will cease working for providers within the City while under 
contract with the City and will not review any submitted reports that were previously created by their firm. These three 
firms are recommended for award to allow staff to assign RF Report reviews to avoid a firm from reviewing their own 
reports.
Contract Terms:
These firms will each enter into separate agreements with the City for approximately two-year terms ending on June 30, 
2026, with up to two optional one-year extensions (with no contract value increases) pending performance review by staff.
Based on the above, staff recommends executing professional consultant agreements with EBI Consulting, OSC 
Engineering, and Waterford Consultants, LLC to provide RF Report review services for a term of two years, with two 
optional one-year extension, for not-to-exceed amounts of $100,000 for each agreement.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The expense of these RF Report review services will be fully cost recoverable as they will be charging to the wireless permit 
deposit accounts as set forth in the most current adopted fee schedule.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
These agreements are not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical 
change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 – Agreement with EBI Consulting
Att 2 – Agreement with OSC Engineering
Att 3 – Agreement with Waterford Consultants

Jason Hallare, Senior Engineer

FOR 
ON-CALL RADIO FREQUENCY REVIEW SERVICES
This Agreement, made and entered into this day of ____________________, by and between 
the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California 
(“CITY”), and EnviroBusiness, Inc. d/b/a EBI Consulting a corporation (“CONSULTANT”), whose address 
is 21 B Street Burlington, MA 01803.
RECITALS:
A.  CITY desires certain radio frequency report review services hereinafter described.
B.  CITY desires to engage CONSULTANT to provide these radio frequency report review 
services by reason of its qualifications and experience for performing such services and CONSULTANT 
has offered to provide the required services on the terms and in the manner set forth herein.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows:
SECTION 1 - SCOPE OF SERVICES
The scope of services to be performed by CONSULTANT under this Agreement is as described in 
Exhibit A to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 2 - DUTIES OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for the professional quality, technical accuracy and 
coordination of all work furnished by CONSULTANT under this Agreement.  CONSULTANT shall, without 
additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in its work.
CONSULTANT represents that it is qualified to furnish the services described under this 
Agreement.
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for employing or engaging all persons necessary to perform 
the services of CONSULTANT.
CONSULTANT agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in 
Municipal Code Chapter 5.92, which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer 
Price Index escalator.
SECTION 3 - DUTIES OF CITY
CITY shall provide pertinent information regarding its requirements for the project.
239 of 671

--- page 240 ---
Professional Services
Page 2 of 11
Rev 9/22
CITY shall examine documents submitted by CONSULTANT and shall render decisions pertaining 
thereto promptly, to avoid unreasonable delay in the progress of CONSULTANT's work.
SECTION 4 - TERM
The services to be performed under this Agreement shall commence upon the execution of the 
Agreement and be completed on June 30, 2026, with up to two (2) optional one-year extensions by the 
CITY.
SECTION 5 - PAYMENT
Payment shall be made by CITY only for services rendered and upon submission of a payment 
request upon completion and CITY approval of the work performed, which approval shall not be 
unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed.  Undisputed fees shall be paid by CITY upon forty-five 
(45) days of receipt of payment request. In consideration for the full performance of the services set 
forth in Exhibit A, CITY agrees to pay CONSULTANT a fee in an amount of $100,000.00, pursuant to 
rates stated in Exhibit B to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 6 - TERMINATION
Without limitation to such rights or remedies as CITY shall otherwise have by law, CITY shall have 
the right to terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the Project for any reason, upon ten (10) days' 
written notice to CONSULTANT.  CONSULTANT agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon 
receipt of said written notice.
CONSULTANT may terminate this Agreement for cause upon giving CITY not less than thirty (30) 
calendar days’ written notice for substantial failure by CITY to perform in accordance with the terms of 
this Agreement.
Upon termination and upon CITY's payment of the amount required to be paid, documents 
become the property of CITY, and CONSULTANT shall transfer them to CITY upon request without 
additional compensation.
SECTION 7 - OWNERSHIP OF DOCUMENTS
All documents prepared by CONSULTANT in the performance of this Agreement, although 
instruments of professional service, are and shall be the property of CITY, whether the project for 
which they are made is executed or not.
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY
All reports and documents prepared by CONSULTANT in connection with the performance of 
this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONSULTANT shall not make any 
such documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed or engaged as 
a subcontractor by CONSULTANT or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.
240 of 671

--- page 241 ---
Professional Services
Page 3 of 11
Rev 9/22
SECTION 9 - INTEREST OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT covenants that it presently has no interest, and shall not acquire any interest, 
direct or indirect, financial or otherwise, which would conflict in any manner or degree with the 
performance of the services under this Agreement.
SECTION 10 - CONSULTANT'S STATUS
It is expressly agreed that in the performance of the professional services required under this 
Agreement, CONSULTANT shall at all times be considered an independent contractor as defined in 
Labor Code Section 3353, under control of the CITY as to the result of the work but not the means by 
which the result is accomplished.  Nothing herein shall be construed to make CONSULTANT an agent or 
employee of CITY while providing services under this Agreement.
SECTION 11 - INDEMNITY
CONSULTANT agrees to hold harmless and indemnify CITY, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, loss, liability, damage, and expense arising 
out of CONSULTANT’s negligence, recklessness or willful misconduct to the maximum extent permitted 
by state law. To the extent permitted by Civil Code section 2782.8, CONSULTANT agrees to defend City, 
its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents against any such claims. 
SECTION 12 - INSURANCE
CONSULTANT shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract and three years 
thereafter (five years for building or major improvements) the insurance specified in Exhibit C to this 
Agreement.
SECTION 13 - NON-ASSIGNABILITY
Both parties hereto recognize that this Agreement is for the personal services of CONSULTANT 
and cannot be transferred, assigned, or subcontracted by CONSULTANT without the prior written 
consent of CITY.
SECTION 14 - RELIANCE UPON PROFESSIONAL
SKILL OF CONSULTANT
It is mutually understood and agreed by and between the parties hereto that CONSULTANT is 
skilled in the professional calling necessary to perform the work agreed to be done under this 
Agreement and that CITY relies upon the skill of CONSULTANT to do and perform the work using that 
degree of care and skill ordinarily exercised under similar circumstances by reputable members of its 
profession practicing in the same locality, and CONSULTANT agrees to thus perform the work.  The 
acceptance of CONSULTANT's work by CITY does not operate as a release of CONSULTANT from said 
obligation.
241 of 671

--- page 242 ---
Professional Services
Page 4 of 11
Rev 9/22
SECTION 15 - WAIVERS
The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this 
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such 
term, covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of 
any other term, condition, ordinance, or law.  The subsequent acceptance by either party of any fee or 
other money which may become due hereunder shall not be deemed to be a waiver of any preceding 
breach or violation by the other party of any term, covenant, or condition of this Agreement or of any 
applicable law or ordinance.
SECTION 16 - SEVERABILITY
If any term or portion of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or otherwise 
unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall 
continue in full force and effect.
SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.
SECTION 18 - NON-DISCRIMINATION
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONSULTANT nor any of its 
subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act.
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.
242 of 671

--- page 243 ---
Professional Services
Page 5 of 11
Rev 9/22
SECTION 20 - LITIGATION
CONSULTANT shall testify at CITY'S request if litigation is brought against CITY in connection 
with CONSULTANT's services under this Agreement.  Unless the action is brought by CONSULTANT, or 
is based upon CONSULTANT's wrongdoing, CITY shall compensate CONSULTANT for preparation for 
testimony, testimony, and travel at CONSULTANT's standard hourly rates at the time of actual 
testimony.
SECTION 21 - NOTICES
All notices hereunder shall be given in writing and mailed, postage prepaid, addressed as 
follows:
To CITY:
Jason Hallare, Senior Engineer
City of San Mateo
330 West 20th Avenue
San Mateo, CA 94403
To CONSULTANT:
EnviroBusiness, Inc.
Attn:  Nolan Previte
21 B Street
Burlington, MA 01803
SECTION 22 - AGREEMENT CONTAINS ALL UNDERSTANDINGS; AMENDMENT
This document represents the entire and integrated agreement between CITY and 
CONSULTANT and supersedes all prior negotiations, representations, and agreements, either written 
or oral.
This document may be amended only by written instrument, signed by both CITY and 
CONSULTANT.
SECTION 23 - AUTHORITY TO ENTER INTO AGREEMENT
CONSULTANT has all requisite power and authority to conduct its business and to execute, 
deliver, and perform the Agreement.  Each party warrants that the individuals who have signed this 
Agreement have the legal power, right, and authority to make this Agreement and to bind each 
respective party.
SECTION 24 - GOVERNING LAW AND VENUE
This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California and, in the event of 
litigation, venue will be in the County of San Mateo.

244 of 671

Professional Services
Page 7 of 11
Rev 9/22
EXHIBIT A
SCOPE OF SERVICES
RF Report Review Services
•
Consultant shall track the total amount work performed for each Wireless Permit under that permit’s specific 
Wireless Permit number (WC#).
•
RF Compliance Reports: 
o
Perform complete review of pre-construction RF compliance reports included with Wireless Permit 
Applications for compliance with Radio Frequency Radiation Exposure Limits of 47 C.F.R. §§ 1.1307(b)(3) 
and 1.1310.
o
Draft comment letters for incomplete or unclear reports that do not conform to City Municipal Code, City 
Standards or FCC regulations for human exposure to RF emissions.
o
Draft approval letters.
o
Review comment or approval letters must be provided within five (5) calendar days.
•
Post-Installation RF Reports: 
o
Perform complete review of post-installation RF reports provided after construction for compliance with 
approved Wireless Permit.
o
Draft comment letters for incomplete or unclear reports that do not conform to City Municipal Code, City 
Standards or FCC regulations or FCC regulations for human exposure to RF emissions.
o
Draft approval letters.
•
Coordination Meetings:
o
Attend coordination meetings as necessary to provide technical advice to staff or seek clarification on 
standards and policies from staff.

EXHIBIT C
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate 
limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or 
the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if 
CONSULTANT has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no 
less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily 
injury or disease. 
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and 
shall be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT.
Other Insurance Provisions
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
Additional Insured Status
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).
Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.
248 of 671

--- page 249 ---
Professional Services
Page 11 of 11
Rev 9/22
Notice of Cancellation
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City. 
CONSULTANT shall provide at least thirty (30) days prior written notice to the City of any cancellation 
of any of the coverages (ten (10) days for non-renewal).
Waiver of Subrogation
CONSULTANT hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said 
CONSULTANT may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  
CONSULTANT agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of 
subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of 
subrogation endorsement from the insurer.  
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions above $100,000 must be declared to and approved by the 
City. The City may require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention 
or provide proof of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense 
expenses within the retention.  
Acceptability of Insurers
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City.
Verification of Coverage
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate

the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if 
CONSULTANT has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no 
less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily 
injury or disease. 
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and 
shall be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT.
Other Insurance Provisions
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
Additional Insured Status
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).
Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.

FOR PROFESSIONAL CONSULTANT SERVICES
FOR 
ON-CALL RADIO FREQUENCY REVIEW SERVICES
This Agreement, made and entered into this day of ____________________, by and between 
the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California 
(“CITY”), and Waterford Consultants, LLC a limited liability company (“CONSULTANT”), whose address 
is 7430 New Technology Way Suite 150, Frederick, MD 21703.
RECITALS:
A.  CITY desires certain radio frequency report review services hereinafter described.
B.  CITY desires to engage CONSULTANT to provide these radio frequency report review 
services by reason of its qualifications and experience for performing such services and CONSULTANT 
has offered to provide the required services on the terms and in the manner set forth herein.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows:
SECTION 1 - SCOPE OF SERVICES
The scope of services to be performed by CONSULTANT under this Agreement is as described in 
Exhibit A to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 2 - DUTIES OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for the professional quality, technical accuracy and 
coordination of all work furnished by CONSULTANT under this Agreement.  CONSULTANT shall, without 
additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in its work.
CONSULTANT represents that it is qualified to furnish the services described under this 
Agreement.
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for employing or engaging all persons necessary to perform 
the services of CONSULTANT.
CONSULTANT agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in 
Municipal Code Chapter 5.92, which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer 
Price Index escalator.
SECTION 3 - DUTIES OF CITY
CITY shall provide pertinent information regarding its requirements for the project.

CITY shall examine documents submitted by CONSULTANT and shall render decisions pertaining 
thereto promptly, to avoid unreasonable delay in the progress of CONSULTANT's work.
SECTION 4 - TERM
The services to be performed under this Agreement shall commence upon the execution of the 
Agreement and be completed on June 30, 2026, with up to two (2) optional one-year extensions by the 
CITY.
SECTION 5 - PAYMENT
Payment shall be made by CITY (net-45 days) only for services rendered and upon submission of

the full performance of the services set forth in Exhibit A, CITY agrees to pay CONSULTANT a fee in an 
amount of $100,000.00, pursuant to rates stated in Exhibit B to this Agreement, attached and 
incorporated by reference.
SECTION 6 - TERMINATION
Without limitation to such rights or remedies as CITY shall otherwise have by law, CITY shall have 
the right to terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the Project for any reason, upon ten (10) days' 
written notice to CONSULTANT.  CONSULTANT agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon 
receipt of said written notice.
Upon termination and upon CITY's payment of the amount required to be paid, documents 
become the property of CITY, and CONSULTANT shall transfer them to CITY upon request without 
additional compensation.
SECTION 7 - OWNERSHIP OF DOCUMENTS
All documents prepared by CONSULTANT in the performance of this Agreement, although 
instruments of professional service, are and shall be the property of CITY, whether the project for 
which they are made is executed or not.
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY
All reports and documents prepared by CONSULTANT in connection with the performance of 
this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONSULTANT shall not make any 
such documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed by 
CONSULTANT or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.

skillful manner, and CONSULTANT agrees to thus perform the work.  The acceptance of CONSULTANT's 
work by CITY does not operate as a release of CONSULTANT from said obligation.

SECTION 15 - WAIVERS
The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this 
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such 
term, covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of 
any other term, condition, ordinance, or law.  The subsequent acceptance by either party of any fee or

breach or violation by the other party of any term, covenant, or condition of this Agreement or of any 
applicable law or ordinance.
SECTION 16 - SEVERABILITY
If any term or portion of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or otherwise 
unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall 
continue in full force and effect.
SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.
SECTION 18 - NON-DISCRIMINATION
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONSULTANT nor any of its 
subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act.
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

EXHIBIT A
SCOPE OF SERVICES
RF Report Review Services
•
Consultant shall track the total amount work performed for each Wireless Permit under that permit’s specific 
Wireless Permit number (WC#).
•
RF Compliance Reports: 
o
Perform complete review of pre-construction RF compliance reports included with Wireless Permit 
Applications for compliance with Radio Frequency Radiation Exposure Limits of 47 C.F.R. §§ 1.1307(b)(3) 
and 1.1310.
o
Draft comment letters for incomplete or unclear reports that do not conform to City Municipal Code, City 
Standards or FCC regulations for human exposure to RF emissions.
o
Draft approval letters.
o
Review comment or approval letters must be provided within five (5) calendar days.
•
Post-Installation RF Reports: 
o
Perform complete review of post-installation RF reports provided after construction for compliance with 
approved Wireless Permit.
o
Draft comment letters for incomplete or unclear reports that do not conform to City Municipal Code, City 
Standards or FCC regulations or FCC regulations for human exposure to RF emissions.
o
Draft approval letters.
•
Coordination Meetings:
o
Attend coordination meetings as necessary to provide technical advice to staff or seek clarification on 
standards and policies from staff.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  16
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 10-4679
File ID: 24-8810
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
June 17, 2024 
SUBJECT:
Storm Drain Master Plan Update - Budget Transfer and Amendment
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to authorize a budget transfer in the amount of $600,000 from the Wet Weather Preparation Activities 
project to the Stormwater System Management and Improvement Program project; approve Amendment No. 1 with 
Schaaf and Wheeler for the Storm Drain Master Plan Update in the amount not to exceed $1,009,190 for a new total 
agreement amount of $1,104,190; establish a contingency reserve of $110,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to 
execute the amendment in substantially the form presented and any future amendments within the contingency amount.
BACKGROUND:
Recent events, including numerous heavy storms and flooding during the 2022-23 rainy season, have highlighted the City’s 
aging infrastructure deficiencies. The City’s Storm Drain Master Plan (SDMP), originally drafted in 2004, has not been 
updated for nearly two decades. Furthermore, due to insufficient funding resources, many of the improvements identified 
in the SDMP for capacity assurance have not been completed. However, the recent passage of the Community Flood and 
Storm Protection Fee provides the City with the financial means to revisit and update the SDMP and begin implementation 
of necessary storm system improvements. 
In January 2024, staff issued a Request for Proposals (RFP) for the SDMP Update, with two proposals submitted by 
qualified engineering consulting firms. After evaluating the proposals and interviewing the consultant teams, staff selected 
Schaaf & Wheeler to provide services for the SDMP Update. Staff determined Schaaf & Wheeler was best qualified for this 
project due to their technical expertise and in-depth experience completing master plans for neighboring jurisdictions.
A portion of the scope of work for the SDMP Update is collection of high-resolution Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) 
data to provide bathymetric and topographic data of creeks/channels and the Marina Lagoon.  To expedite collection of 
this data at the Marina Lagoon to support the City’s separate Marina Lagoon Spot Dredging Project, staff established an 
initial contract with Schaaf & Wheeler in an amount not to exceed $95,000 to collect LiDAR data for the Marina Lagoon.  
Staff is now requesting Council approval of Amendment No. 1 to the initial contract in an amount of $1,009,190 to 
incorporate the full scope of services for the SDMP Update, including additional LiDAR data collection beyond the Marina 
Lagoon. Staff also recommends establishing a 10% contingency reserve to allow for scope modifications to address 
unknowns as the implementation of the SDMP proceeds. The term of the existing agreement is through December 31, 
2025, unless amended, which is anticipated to be sufficient for completing the SDMP Update.
On November 6, 2023, Council allocated $600,000 in General Funds for LiDAR data acquisition as part of a larger $3 million 
allocation under the Wet Weather Preparation Activities Project. Implementation of LiDAR data acquisition work, including 
270 of 671

--- page 271 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
collection and interpretation of the data, has been incorporated into Schaaf & Wheeler’s SDMP scope of work and is at a 
lower cost than staff originally anticipated (approximately $150,000). Recognizing the importance of updating the SDMP 
quickly and that the new Community Flood and Storm Protection Fee revenues will not start being collected until early 
2025, staff is requesting a budget transfer authorization to move the full amount of $600,000 from the Wet Weather 
Preparation Activities project budget to the Stormwater System Management Improvement Program project to fund both 
LiDAR costs and a portion of the overall SDMP Update cost. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Currently, there is an estimated available project account balance of $425,000 in the Stormwater System Management and 
Improvement Program (46W002). The proposed resolution would authorize a transfer in the Capital Project Fund in the 
amount of $600,000 from the Wet Weather Preparation Activities (46C006) project to the Stormwater System 
Management and Improvement Program (46W002) project, providing sufficient budget to fund this amendment.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This action is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in 
direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 - Proposed Resolution
Att 2 - Amendment No. 1

Jimmy Vo, Senior Engineer
jvo@cityofsanmateo.org
(650) 522-7319

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. ____ (2024)
AUTHORIZING A BUDGET TRANSFER IN THE CAPITAL PROJECT FUND OF $600,000 FROM WET 
WEATHER PREPARATION ACTIVITIES PROJECT TO THE STORMWATER SYSTEM MANAGEMENT AND 
IMPROVEMENT PROGRAM PROJECT 
WHEREAS, the Council considers mid-year budget balancing through adjustments, appropriations, 
designations and transfers to adjust the current-year budget to meet anticipated changes in departmental 
budgetary needs; and
WHEREAS, as detailed in the accompanying Administrative Report, a budget transfer and appropriation 
is necessary to accomplish the City’s day-to-day business to support its goals and programs and to provide for 
anticipated additional expenditures; and
WHEREAS, amendments to the Adopted 2023-2024 Budget are necessary to address funding of those 
items; and
WHEREAS, the Stormwater System Management and Improvement Program (46W002) does not have 
enough remaining available budget to cover the estimated $1,010,000 in funding needed for the Storm Drain 
Master Plan Update; and
WHEREAS, $600,000 in funds from the Wet Weather Preparation Activities (46C006) budget is available 
to be transferred.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, RESOLVES that:
1.
This action is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity 
that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines 
Section 15378(b)(5).)
2.
A transfer in the Capital Project Fund totaling $600,000 from the Wet Weather Preparation Activities 
Project (46C006) to the Stormwater System Management and Improvement Program Project 
(46W002) is authorized.

EXHIBIT A1 
Schaaf & Wheeler 
Scope of Work 
Task 1: Project Management 
This task includes coordination with City staff and other stakeholders to advance the work, 
management strategies, budgeting, invoicing, schedule tracking and quality control. Specific 
tasks include:  
 
Kick-off meeting 
 
Regular project meetings (assume 20 meetings at up to 2 hours each) 
 
Special meetings including correspondence with the San Francisco PUC 
 
Manage project schedule 
 
Monthly billing and reporting 
 
Presentations (e.g. City Council presentations, City staff presentations, Special 
commission presentations) 
 
Quality assurance and quality control comment tracking spreadsheet 
 
Document management within SharePoint, including survey files, engineering 
computations, computer models, GIS and mapping, documented assumptions, working 
drawings, meeting minutes, correspondence and digital images.  
Task 1: Deliverables: 
 
Project Meeting Agendas and Minutes 
 
Monthly Invoices and Progress Reports 
 
Project Schedule and updates 
Task 2: Review and Evaluate Existing Data 
Consultant will review readily available data and information related to the drainage and flood 
control systems within the City of San Mateo and relevant tributary areas to identify data gaps 
and inconsistencies. These data may include GIS geodatabases, record drawings, land use and 
zoning plans, survey data, drainage reports for both public works and private development 
projects, and regional water resources data.  
The following is a partial list of potential documents that will be evaluated:  
 
Operating and maintenance records  
 
City zoning plan 
 
City land use mapping 
 
City GIS geodata 
 
FEMA effective and preliminary FIS and FIRM 
 
LOMCs 
 
CDEC and USGS gage data 
 
California sea-level rise guidance 
 
Climate change precipitation data and reports  
 
SFPUC operations data and plans 
 
Drainage design standards 
 
Hydrologic methodologies 
 
Applicable regional and citywide permits related to storm drainage and flooding 
 
Existing hydraulic models  
 
2002 Flood Management Strategies report 
 
2004 Storm Drain Master Plan 
 
2019 Marina Lagoon Preliminary Dredging Assessment

Task 2: Deliverables: 
 
Technical Memorandum summarizing the documents reviewed, identified data gaps, and 
inconsistencies. 
Task 3: Topographic and Geometric Information 
The aerial extent and scope of topographic surveying will be discussed in detail before work 
starts. Consultant will develop a comprehensive bare earth citywide surface that will include 
creek geometries based on field surveys along with the most current County of San Mateo 
LiDAR topography in overbank areas. These ground surfaces will be utilized for developing 
water levels in drainage channels, detention basins, creeks, and Marina Lagoon.  
Note: Bathymetric information for Marina Lagoon will be collected under separate contract but 
available for use in this scope of work. 
Additional surveys and/or LiDAR topography may be required. Consultant will review the 
following data sources of topography to identify remaining topographic data gaps:  
 
LiDAR topography obtained by the County of San Mateo in 2017 
 
2023 County of San Mateo LiDAR (if completed) 
 
An aerial survey of the Bayfront obtained in 2001 
 
Field surveys of channel cross sections for the entire lengths of 16th Avenue Channel, 
19th Avenue Channel, and Laurel Creek obtained in 2002 
 
Field surveys of channel cross sections for San Mateo Creek from Highway 101 to San 
Francisco Bay obtained in 2002  
 
FEMA archive cross sections for San Mateo Creek upstream of Highway 101 circa 1996  
 
Marina Lagoon Bathymetry obtained by Moffat Nickel in 2017 
The project surveyor, Kier & Wright, will survey hydraulic structures on San Mateo Creek, 19th 
Avenue Channel, 16th Avenue Channel, and Laurel Creek within city limits. Each structure, 
typically culvert or bridge, will be surveyed at the upstream and downstream faces, along with 
sections 50-feet and 100-feet offset from the structure.  The surveyor will also measure 
dimensions of each hydraulic structure (diameter, height, pier radius). 
Horizontal and vertical datums on which master planning work will be based will be established 
by the City at project kickoff, and the datums will not change once established. 
Task 3: Deliverables: 
 
 Digital Elevation Model 
 
Field survey notes and hydraulic structure diagrams 
 
Creek channel cross sections in City-requested formats 
 
Collected and developed GIS files 
 
276 of 671

--- page 277 ---
EXHIBIT A1 
Schaaf & Wheeler 
Task 4: Data Collection and Condition Assessment 
Following review and evaluation of existing data, Consultant will develop an approach and set of 
criteria to evaluate the condition of the City's storm drainage system facilities and develop a list 
of facilities recommended for inspection to be submitted as a Condition Assessment Plan to the 
City for review. Once approved, Consultant will implement and manage the condition 
assessment effort. Key information generated from this task will be shared with the City for 
incorporation into the City's geodatabase.  
Consultant will utilize office and field methods to establish reasonable assumptions for the 
various pipe and system conditions within the storm drainage network. Where needed, a portion 
of the pipe segments will require field reconnaissance to perform visual inspections, photo 
documentation, and measurements. In addition, a smaller subset of critical or concerning pipes 
will require a more detailed review involving closed-circuit television (CCTV) and potentially 
pipeline flushing.  
Consultant will evaluate the integrity of the system based on the CCTV data, size, pipe material, 
elevation/inverts, and age of the distribution system. The NASSCO rating system will be utilized 
for categorizing system issues. Based on the findings, the assessment will recommend 
improvements as part of the short- and long-term CIP.  
Since condition assessment priorities and available access to the facilities are unknown, CCTV 
will also be scoped based on a time-and-materials basis rather than a physical distance. As an 
allowance, Consultant will spend up to 20 days in the field collecting data at prioritized locations. 
The team’s CCTV firm, Presidio Systems, will spend up to 30 days in the field, also at prioritized 
locations. 
Pump Station Condition Assessments 
Each of the 10 stormwater pump stations will be visited by the project team, along with City 
operations staff. The team will evaluate the effectiveness and capacity of the current stormwater 
pump stations and outline future improvements, including recommended replacement intervals.  
Task 4: Deliverables: 
 
Draft and Final Condition Assessment Plan 
 
GIS Datafiles 
 
CCTV Inspection Data, Video Recordings, and Reports 
 
Pump Station Inspection Reports 
Task 5: Hydraulic Model Update  
Before updating hydrologic and hydraulic models, Consultant will provide a technical memo 
clarifying the status of hydraulic models, propose storm events to be used for modeling, and 
propose alternative methodologies for City review and consideration.  
Consultant will develop an updated hydrologic and hydraulic model of the City’s flood control 
and drainage systems. Laurel Creek, 19th Avenue Channel, 16th Avenue Channel, and San 
Mateo Creek systems will be modeled in HEC-RAS with 1-D and 2-D components.  
A MIKE+ model of the drainage networks will include City-owned drainage systems 12-inches in 
diameter and larger, pump stations, lagoons, and detention facilities including the two on Laurel 
Creek and East Laurel Creek. Private drainage systems and water quality basins will be 
277 of 671

--- page 278 ---
EXHIBIT A1 
Schaaf & Wheeler 
modeled if found to be significant. Creek levels from the HEC-RAS models will be utilized as 
boundary conditions for the pipe network and 2D flow models. 
The models will be run for the 2-year, 10-year and 100-year events. Climate change adaptation 
will also be incorporated as coordinated with the City. Watershed hydrology will be modeled with 
HEC-HMS. Consultant will develop a design storm pattern for use in all modelling exercises.  
Consultant will identify system components that do not meet the established level of service 
standards and design a program of improvement so the standard can be met. Up to two (2) 
alternatives will be developed for the five (5) highest priority projects and critical facilities. 
Programs of improvement and critical facility alternatives will be vetted for performance using 
the updated hydraulic models. 
Climate Change Adaptation 
The Stormwater Master Plan will evaluate climate change resiliency related to the established 
level of service criteria and the 2-year, 10-year, and 100-year results. Projected future changes 
in precipitation and San Francisco Bay water levels will be applied to the hydraulic  models to  
evaluate direct impacts to potential CIP programs.  
Model Update Workshop 
Consultant will lead a workshop after completing Task 5 for the City’s technical engineering staff 
to communicate model development, scenarios evaluated, limitations, and assumptions.  
Task 5: Deliverables: 
 
Technical memo outlining proposed modeling alternatives, etc. 
 
Updated Hydrologic and Hydraulic Model in MIKE+ and HEC-RAS 
 
Technical workshop materials 
Task 6: Identify and Prioritize Capital Improvements  
Consultant will utilize modeling results and condition assessments to determine and map level 
of service limitations within in the existing storm drainage and flood control system. Remedies 
for system issues will be developed on a coarse level along with initial prioritization. Priorities 
will be based on life safety and property damage potential. Consultant will present these initial 
capital improvement projects to the City for review and comment.  
Once the City and Consultant have established the list of the most critical projects, priority 
rankings and costs will be applied to all identified CIPs. Based on identified funding sources, a 
CIP schedule will be developed. The planning horizons will likely be immediate, 5-year, 10-year, 
and 20-year, or as otherwise established with the City.  
Consultant will develop a detailed description of the highest priority improvement projects, along 
with details for phasing, capital cost requirements, permitting requirements, ROW/easement 
requirements, cost estimates that incorporate escalation costs, and an implementation 
schedule. Consultant will identify project interfaces or interdependences. For example, are there 
specific projects or groups of projects which need to be implemented together or are sequence 
dependent? Capital project bundles and sequences will be designed to prevent induced flooding 
elsewhere. 
Consultant will participate in the community engagement process, which will be performed by 
the City and others. 
278 of 671

--- page 279 ---
EXHIBIT A1 
Schaaf & Wheeler 
Task 6: Deliverables: 
 
Technical Memorandum identifying project prioritization methodology including proposed 
scoring parameters and weighting percentages. 
Task 7: Operation, Maintenance, and NPDES 
The City spends a significant amount of its stormwater budget on keeping the drainage systems 
operating effectively. Consultant will work with City staff to document the current operations and 
maintenance procedures and look for opportunities to improve the system. Resources, staff and 
equipment, and allocations for various tasks will be summarized. Staff input into how to improve 
the program will be critical in providing improvements. 
Task 7: Deliverables: 
 
Technical Memorandum summarizing current operations and maintenance procedures 
and recommendations for improvements. 
Task 8: Funding/Financial Analysis  
Consultant will coordinate with the City to update the 2023 Stormwater Fee projects. This effort 
will include updating cost estimates, re-prioritizing CIPs, and adjusting the CIP schedule. 
Consultant will help develop a method of prioritizing, funding, and financing the updated CIPs 
based on the new Stormwater Master Plan results. We will utilize our breadth of experience and 
relationships with others to seek outside short- and long-term funding, using existing funding as 
leverage through grants, loans, partnership, etc. offered by nearby local, state, and federal 
agencies.  
If the City engages a financial specialist for this work outside of this contract, Consultant will 
coordinate with the City on that engagement as part of the work covered under this agreement. 
Task 8: Deliverables: 
 
Technical Memorandum describing identified outside funding opportunities for storm 
drain capital improvements and ongoing maintenance. 
 
Up to two meetings with City-engaged financial specialist. 
 
In conjunction with Task 10, provide summary project sheets including project 
description, project need, capital budget, and other information pertinent to outside 
funding sources. 
Task 9: Develop Draft and Final Stormwater Master Plan Update Documents 
The Stormwater Master Plan documents will summarize the existing storm system, outline the 
system’s current performance, detail areas currently subject to flooding, list and prioritize 
improvement projects, include a cost estimate for all improvements, and summarize all technical 
methodologies and model results. The report will include both the operations needs and the 
condition assessment needs. A draft schedule of CIPs and a funding strategy will be included.  
Consultant will submit a Draft Stormwater Master Plan to the City for their review prior to hosting 
a meeting with the City to review comments and record feedback. This meeting may need to be 
broken into sessions to ensure critical staff can attend.  
279 of 671

--- page 280 ---
EXHIBIT A1 
Schaaf & Wheeler 
The City will provide one consolidated list of comments to Consultant, who will address said 
comments and finalize the Stormwater Master Plan document. Consultant will also provide a 
signed and stamped Final Engineer’s Report for use in financial program analysis if requested. 
In addition, the Final Stormwater Master Plan will define the purpose, scope, and limitations of 
the update, and it will include a list of all supporting material. The Stormwater Master Plan 
update shall incorporate the technical memos developed under preceding tasks into the master 
plan document text. 
Consultant will present summaries of findings at one Council meeting and four (4) community 
meetings and/or workshops.  
Task 9: Deliverables: 
 
Draft and Final Stormwater Master Plan 
 
Council and community meeting/workshop materials. 
Task 10: Conceptual Designs  
In collaboration with the City, Consultant will identify up to five (5) of highest priority projects 
crucial to successful CIP implementation and prepare conceptual designs and capital cost 
estimates for these projects, which will serve as the basis for more detailed planning and 
eventual implementation. These conceptual designs are intended to be comprehensive enough 
to demonstrate the viability and potential impact of the proposed improvements. By having 
these plans at a planning level of detail, the City can enhance its competitiveness when seeking 
funding opportunities through grants and other sources.

SUBJECT:
Partial Excess General Liability Insurance Settlement Offer for Covid-19 Business Interruption for Parks and Recreation - 
Authorization
RECOMMENDATION:
Authorize the City Attorney to take all necessary steps and execute appropriate documents to accept a partial excess 
general liability settlement offer from the City’s excess general liability insurance carrier, McLarens, for fiscal year 2019-
2020, in the amount of $54,878, for loss of revenue and other expenses related to COVID-19 for the Parks and Recreation 
department. In addition, authorize the City Attorney to execute all required general liability insurance documents to 
recover the loss for all future settlement offers related to this claim.
BACKGROUND:
On March 16, 2020, the City Council adopted Resolution No. 25 (2020) and proclaimed the existence of a local emergency 
to protect the safety of persons and property due to the threat caused by the spread of Covid-19. A shelter-in-place was 
declared and the Parks and Recreation facilities were closed and activities were cancelled. As a result, the Parks and 
Recreation department experienced a loss of revenue, from March 13, 2020, to May 3, 2020, in the amount of 
$827,540.35.
Total Revenue Loss March 13th through to May 3rd.
Recreation Registration Cancellations
 $  424,353.46 
Facility Reservations King
 $    51,833.75 
Facility Reservations Seniors
 $       5,403.75 
Facility Reservations Beresford
 $    10,669.50 
Facility Reservations Fields
 $       4,062.00 
Golf - Rounds
 $  307,751.82 
Golf - Pro Shop
 $       3,466.08 
Golf - Restaurant Lease
 $    20,000.00 
 $  827,540.35
In August 2020, the City Attorney coordinated with the City’s insurance broker, Alliant, to notify and file a claim with the 
City’s excess general liability insurance carrier, McLarens.  The claim was filed on behalf of the City’s Parks and Recreation 
department which experienced a loss in revenue. The claim was classified as a ”Business Interruption“ as a result of the 
loss of revenue that occurred between March 13 - May 3, 2020, for the shut-down and cancellation of Parks and 
282 of 671

--- page 283 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Recreations facilities and activities.
McLarens, one of the City’s excess general liability insurance carriers for fiscal year 2019-2020 sent a letter to the City 
Attorney in May 2024 informing them of a partial excess general liability insurance settlement available due to the 
business interruption that occurred in 2020. The partial settlement is for the amount of $54,878. McLarens and their 
underwriters agreed that the claim qualifies for a pro-rata share of the $10.0 million sublimit. McLarens reported that 
there were roughly 100 claims that qualified for consideration with perhaps 10 additional claims still outstanding. The 
City’s insurance underwriters for the claim year 2019-2020 have agreed to issue a partial payment in the amount of 
$54,878 to the City. McLarens requests that a Partial Master Proof of Loss be signed by an authorized representative of the 
City of San Mateo and returned for handling.
 
Until all claims close, McLarens will not be in a position to undertake a final accounting. There is a possibility that further 
settlement funds may be available in the future as a result of this claim.
Staff is recommending that Council authorize the City Attorney to execute the Partial Master Proof of Loss and return to 
McLarens so that the City may receive $54,878 for this partial settlement offer. In addition, staff is recommending that 
Council authorize the City Attorney to execute any future general liability insurance settlement documentation related to 
the business interruption claim for the fiscal year 2019-2020 and loss of revenue for the Parks and Recreation department.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Budget impact would be positive for the Parks and Recreation department as the partial settlement of $54,878 would be 
deposited into a Parks and Recreation fund available for future revenue count. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This approval is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result 
in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
None

SUBJECT:
Police Department Trailer Camera Purchase - Change Order 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve a change order in the amount of $10,568.25 to the Purchase Order with Mobile Pro Systems for the purchase of 
two grant-funded surveillance camera trailers; authorize the Chief of Police to issue change orders of up to $50,000 
cumulatively; and authorize the City Manager to issue change orders in excess of $50,000 but not to exceed $100,000 
cumulatively.
BACKGROUND:
On March 18, 2024 the City Council authorized a number of actions related to San Mateo’s participation in the Organized 
Retail Theft Prevention Grant Program. Included in those actions was the approval to purchase two surveillance camera 
trailers in the amount of $114,700 using grant funds. Due to an administrative oversight, the dollar amount authorized for 
that purchase did not include tax. The total amount of the purchase, including tax is $125,268.25 and will be fully offset by 
grant reimbursement. Staff requests approval of a change order in the amount of $10,568.25 to complete the purchase; 
and requests Council authorization to clearly authorize the Chief of Police to issue any future change orders up to $50,000 
cumulatively; and the City Manager to issue any future change orders in excess of $50,000 but not to exceed $100,000 
cumulatively, as provided in the City the Purchasing Policy. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
This purchase will be fully offset by grant reimbursement resulting in no net financial impact to the general fund.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This action is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in 
direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Account Number: 82-4683
File ID: 24-8841
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Parks and Recreation Department
MEETING DATE:
June 17, 2024 
SUBJECT:
Central Recreation Center Self-Help for the Elderly – Lease Amendment
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing method; approve a lease amendment to extend the term of the 
lease with Self-Help for the Elderly for the use of Central Recreation Center; authorize the City Manager to execute the 
lease amendment in substantially the form presented. 
BACKGROUND:
Since 1991 the Central Recreation Center has been leased to Self-Help for the Elderly (Self Help), a non-profit specializing 
in the delivery of programs for older adults with special emphasis on meeting the needs of Asian seniors. The current 
lease, which was entered into on June 15, 2016 has an expiration date of June 30, 2025.
The department is currently executing Phase 1 of the Central Park Master Plan, which includes upgrades to the playground 
and new restrooms. The existing recreation center is not projected to be demolished until Phase 3 which the department 
does not expect to undertake in the near future. However, due to the implementation of the Central Park Master Plan, 
Self-Help expressed a desire to extend the term of the lease in order to address the longer-term interests of their desired 
service provision in San Mateo County. 
Following several months of discussion, staff and Self-Help have agreed to extend the term by five (5) years from June 30, 
2025, to June 30, 2030, and to provide for five (5) mutual one-year extension options until June 30, 2035, by mutual 
written consent to be executed prior to the expiration of the then-current term. All other terms of the lease will remain 
the same, including the annual rent of one dollar and zero cents ($1.00). The lease amendment continues the practice of 
Self Help reimbursing the City for PG&E (utility) costs. This amount fluctuates yearly; however, in FY 2023-2024 the year-
to-date amount is approximately $17,118. In addition, Self-Help may choose to purchase custodial supplies at the City’s 
cost, on a reimbursement basis.
The Municipal Code typically requires that lease agreements be put out for competitive bid; however, in accordance with 
Municipal Code sections 3.60.050(b) and 3.60.070(h)(3), the City can make findings that support not putting leases out to 
bid. In this case, calling for competitive bids would be impracticable, because the City wishes to accommodate the existing 
tenant for an extension of its lease.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The lease terms result in the loss of approximately $24,000 in revenue per year due to limited availability for public rentals 
compared to other recreation facilities managed by internal staff. Given that the annual revenue from recreation programs 
and services is approximately $4 million, the loss of this revenue represents less than 1% of the total revenue collected.
286 of 671

--- page 287 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This lease amendment is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, 
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. ____ (2024)
APPROVING AN ALTERNATIVE PURCHASING METHOD FOR THE LEASE AMENDMENT WITH SELF-HELP FOR THE 
ELDERLY FOR THE USE OF CENTRAL RECREATION CENTER
WHEREAS, Self Help For the Elderly has an existing lease with City for the Central Recreation Center, 
which was executed on July 1, 2016, and terminates on June 30, 2025 (the “Existing Lease”); and
WHEREAS, the parties wish to amend the Existing Lease term to end on June 30, 2030 and to provide for 
five (5) mutual one-year optional extensions until June 30, 2035; and
WHEREAS, it is in the City's best interest to dispense with competitive bidding for the Central Recreation 
Center, because the City wishes to accommodate the existing tenant; and
WHEREAS, in accordance with Municipal Code section 3.60.050(b), calling for competitive bids would be 
impracticable because the City wishes to accommodate the existing tenant; and
WHEREAS, in accordance with Municipal Code section 3.60.070(h)(3), the City Council approves of staff's 
negotiations with the existing tenant in lieu of competitive bidding.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY FINDS AND 
RESOLVES that:
1.
This lease amendment is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of 
the operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public 
or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or 
no expansion of use (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301).
2.
In accordance with Municipal Code section 3.60.050(b), calling for competitive bids would be 
impracticable, because the City wishes to accommodate the existing tenant, Self Help for the Elderly 
which has leased the premises since 1991.
3.
In accordance with Municipal Code section 3.60.070(h)(3), the City Council approves staff's negotiations 
with the existing tenant in lieu of competitive bidding for the reasons stated above.

Mateo; and
WHEREAS, City and Lessee wish to amend the Agreement to extend the term by five (5) years from June 
30, 2025, to June 30, 2030, and to provide for five (5) mutual one-year extension options.
NOW, THEREFORE, the Parties agree as follows:
1.
Section C.2 of the Agreement is amended to read as follows:
TERM. The term of this Agreement shall be from July 1, 2016 to June 30, 2030 (“Term"). The Parties may 
extend the term of this Agreement for five (5) additional one-year periods, until June 30, 2035, by 
mutual written consent to be executed prior to the expiration of the then-current term.  However, the 
parties agree to terminate this Agreement earlier if Lessee finds another location from which to provide 
programs and services to the San Mateo community. Lessee will notify City in writing specifying the date 
on which Lessee will vacate the Center. This Agreement will terminate on the date specified in Tenant’s 
notice.  
2.
All other terms and conditions of the Lease shall remain the same.

SUBJECT:
Department of Information Technology – Classification Updates
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to correct the salary range for the classification of Customer Service Manager.
BACKGROUND:
On April 17, 2023, Council approved the reclassification of Technical Support Supervisor to a new classification Customer 
Service Manager and the recommendation to set the monthly salary range for Customer Service Manager to match the 
salary range of the GIS Coordinator classification. However, the salary schedule attached to the Resolution that Council 
approved did not have the correct salary range. The Human Resources Department requests to correct the monthly salary 
range for the Customer Service Manager classification to match the GIS Coordinator classification at $11,293.94 - 
$13,468.34.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The correction for the salary schedule of the Customer Service Manager will have no budget impact, as the position has 
been budgeted with the correct salary schedule.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
These classification updates are not subject to CEQA, because they will not cause a physical change in the environment. 
(CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

SUBJECT:
Recruitment Services – Change Order
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Change Order No. 2 to the contract with It's Personnel Consulting for the purpose of professional recruitment 
services in the amount of $60,000.00 for a new contract total of $150,000.00; and authorize the City Manager to execute 
the change order in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
The FY 2023-24 agreement with It’s Personnel Consulting to conduct professional recruitment services had an original 
Purchase Order (PO) amount of $60,000.00. In November 2023, the City retained the services of It’s Personnel to conduct 
recruitment services as part of the recruitment blitz to fill critical vacancies. As of July 1, 2023, It’s Personnel has conducted 
55 recruitments for the City of San Mateo while the HR recruitment staff participate in the Workday implementation. In 
March 2024, the PO amount was increased to $90,000.00 through Change Order No. 1 with City Manager approval. 
Currently, $1,352.85 remains on the PO with approximate incurred expenses to be paid in the amount of $26,317.27 for 
recruitment services through April 2024. HR is requesting to increase the PO amount by $60,000.00 for a total contract of 
$150,000.00 for recruitment services through June 30, 2024.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The recruitment services conducted by It’s Personnel Consulting are paid for by the recruiting department and there is 
sufficient funding within the department budgets to cover the additional costs.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This change order is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not 
result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

SUBJECT:
Boards and Commissions Appointment Subcommittee – Appointment Recommendation
RECOMMENDATION:

2026, and place Charles Hansen and Lamoin Werlein-Jaén on the eligibility list for six months, should another vacancy on 
the Commission occur. Appoint Edward “Rich” Kranz to the Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission for a full-term 
beginning June 17, 2024, through June 10, 2028, and place Theodore McKinnon and Segio Zygmunt on the eligibility list for 
six months, should another vacancy on the Commission occur. Appoint Annie Tsai to the Library Board for a partial term 
from June 17, 2024, through June 10, 2025, and place Lindsey Martinson and Alison Joly on the eligibility list for six 
months, should another vacancy on the Board occur. Appoint Adam Klafter to the Planning Commission for a full-term

Agenda Number:  23
Section Name: PUBLIC HEARING
