MEETING DATE:
June 17, 2024 
SUBJECT:
Downtown Business Improvement Area – Annual Renewal
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to Levy the Annual Assessments for the Downtown Business Improvement Area for fiscal year 2024-25.
BACKGROUND:
The Downtown Business Improvement Area (DBIA) was formed in 1986 for the purpose of improving Downtown San 
Mateo and promoting businesses within the area. The Downtown San Mateo Association (DSMA) has served as the DBIA 
administrator since its formation. The DSMA provides the City Council with a report of activities and expenditures for the 
previous fiscal year and proposes an assessment schedule for the upcoming fiscal year, as required by the California Street 
and Highways Code Section 36533. In May 2024, the DSMA submitted its annual report, incorporating a summary of fiscal 
year 2023-24 activities and proposed program and budget for fiscal year 2024-25 (included as Attachment 2). 
Before City Council may act on the DSMA’s request to renew the DBIA assessments, the request must be considered at two 
City Council meetings. At the last meeting on June 3, 2024, the City Council accepted the DSMA’s annual report, adopted a 
Resolution of Intent to Levy the Annual Assessment, and set a public hearing date for June 17, 2024. At the second 
meeting, the City Council is asked to adopt the Resolution to levy the annual assessment (included as Attachment 1).
Annual Assessment 
This year marks the first time the DSMA has had a full-time Executive Director, Mackenzie Jakoubek, who brings 15 years 
of experience in nonprofit management, customer service, and event planning. After the resignation of the previous 
Executive Director in 2017, a group of volunteer business owners, with administrative support from the City, managed the 
DSMA. Since assuming her role as Executive Director, Ms. Jakoubek has engaged with Downtown businesses and 
community allies, and supported efforts of partner organizations such as the San Mateo Chamber of Commerce and the 
City of San Mateo to enhance and enliven the Downtown environment.
The hiring of a new executive director was made possible through the DSMA's efforts to amend assessment rates over 
several years. In 2021, DSMA hired NBS, a consultancy specializing in forming special financial districts to study a new rate 
structure to better reflect and equitably share costs among Downtown businesses, as DBIA fees had remained flat since 
2009. Following the study, DSMA engaged CivicMic to facilitate community outreach, including multilingual mailings, 
online meetings, and a survey about DSMA. Feedback showed businesses were aware of and valued DSMA's services, and 
they highlighted the goals of cleanliness, beautification, events, marketing, and the need to hire an Executive Director as 
priorities. After gathering input, DSMA requested the City Council amend the DBIA levy, and held a public hearing on 
November 7, 2022. In response to feedback from small and medium-sized businesses, DSMA proposed lower rate category 
for sole proprietors and medium-sized businesses with 6 to 15 employees at the Council meeting. The modified rates were 
303 of 671

--- page 304 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
adopted and became effective on July 1, 2023.
For the fiscal year 2024-25, the DSMA expects to generate approximately $382k from DBIA assessments, aligning with its 
budget. Additionally, it plans to allocate $48,000 for hosting community-building events, $101,000 for cleaning services, 
and $63,000 for marketing. The remaining funds will cover personnel and consulting services. The DSMA aims to maintain 
a reserve of $211k. The DSMA shares an office with the Downtown Coordinator and does not utilize any City equipment or 
furniture. The shared space allows the DSMA to redirect savings towards programs and events. Additionally, the shared 
office facilitates a closer relationship between the City and the DSMA, enhancing collaboration and communication.
While some stakeholders have voiced dissent in having to participate in the fees, the majority have noticed the benefits of 
having increased events, enhanced marketing, and vibrancy in the Downtown. At the June 17, 2024 public hearing, 
Mackenzie Jakoubek, Executive Director of the DSMA will present an overview of the DSMA’s Annual Report and will be 
able to address any questions from the Council and public. 
According to Streets and Highways Code Section 36535, if written protests are submitted by business owners in the DBIA, 
and these protests collectively represent 50% or more of the proposed assessments, no additional actions to impose the 
assessments will occur for a period of one year. Any written protests received will be provided at the June 17, 2024 Council 
meeting.
Should Council adopt the resolution levying the proposed annual Downtown Business Improvement Area assessment, the 
Finance Department will include the 2024-25 DBIA assessments in all the City business tax license fees for businesses 
located within the boundaries of the DBIA.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The levying of the annual DBIA assessment does not have an impact on the General Fund. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in 
direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting notice requirements were met. Pursuant to California Streets and Highways Code Section 36534, a notice for 
the public hearing was published in the San Mateo Daily Journal at least seven (7) days prior to the June 17, 2024 City 
Council hearing.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO.____ (2024)
LEVYING THE ANNUAL ASSESSMENT (2024-2025) 
FOR THE DOWNTOWN BUSINESS IMPROVEMENT AREA
WHEREAS, the City Council established the Downtown Business Improvement Area (DBIA) pursuant to  
Streets and Highways Code Section 36500, et seq.; and
WHEREAS, the Downtown San Mateo Association, in conjunction with City staff acts as the advisory 
board to the City Council for the DBIA; and 
WHEREAS, the City Council has received and reviewed the annual report submitted by the Downtown
San Mateo Association and hereby confirms said Association as the advisory board for the DBIA; and
WHEREAS, a public hearing was held after being duly noticed for a time and place specified in the notice 
of intention; and
WHEREAS, all protests, either written or otherwise to the assessments proposed in the report were 
heard and considered at the public hearing; and
WHEREAS, Streets and Highways Code section 36535(b) authorizes the City Council, upon the conclusion 
of the public hearing, to order changes in any of the matters provided in the report, including changes in 
the proposed assessments; and
WHEREAS, staff recommends that the City Council approve the proposed assessments; and
WHEREAS, this project is exempt from California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) requirements
because it is an administrative activity that will not have a significant effect on the environment per
CEQA Guidelines section 15378(b)(5)).
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES 
that:
1.
The City Council confirms that a majority protest, within the meaning of Streets and 
Highways Code Section 36525, was not made with regard to the proposed assessments.
2.
The City Council approves the assessments set forth in the “Proposed Annual Assessment 
Schedule 2024-2025,” attached hereto as Exhibit A, for the Downtown Business Area set 
forth in the “DBIA Boundary Map,” attached hereto as Exhibit B.
3.
The City Council confirms the annual report submitted by the Downtown San Mateo 
Association as filed and attached to the June 3, 2024, Administrative Report.
4.
The City Council authorizes the levying and collection of the 2024-2025 annual DBIA 
assessments in the amounts shown in the “Proposed Annual Assessment Schedule 2024-
2025, attached hereto as Exhibit A for the Downtown Business Area set forth in the “DBIA 
Boundary Map,” attached hereto as Exhibit B.

Proposed Annual Assessment Schedule FY 2024-2025 (Continued)
Retail businesses include but is not limited to businesses that have goods and foods for sale including 
automotive vehicles and parts dealers, home furnishing stores, jewelers, electronics and appliance 
stores, building material and garden equipment and supplies dealers, food and beverage stores, 
fullserve restaurants, limited services restaurants, fast food restaurants, cafeterias, dessert and snack 
establishments, bars, gasoline stations, clothing and clothing accessories, sporting goods, hobby stores, 
music instruments, book stores, general merchandise store, wireless phone stores and similar.
Professional businesses include but is not limited to businesses that offer services that are highly 
specialized and typically require licensing and regulations. Professional service providers and 
information-based businesses such as software publishers, data processing, network hosting and related 
services, internet publishing and broadcasting and web search portals, venture capital, insurance 
agencies and brokerage, real estate agents and brokers, offices of lawyers, accounting, architectural, 
engineering advertising, translation and interpretation services, veterinary services, offices of physicians, 
offices of dentists, other health practitioners, and similar.
All businesses classified as Professional shall pay an annual assessment based on the number of fulltime 
equivalency employees.
Sole Proprietor is defined as a self-employed individual who works for themselves without hiring any 
employees.
Service businesses include but is not limited to providers of personal and business services such as hair 
salons, nail salons, lock smiths, dry cleaners, auto service and maintenance shops, gyms, vacuum repair 
shops, educational, childcare, electronic equipment repair, personal care services, and similar.
All businesses classified as Service shall pay an annual assessment based on the number of full-time 
equivalency employees.
Financial businesses include but not limited to businesses in the financial industry such as money 
management, payments, digital banking, banks, savings and loan companies, credit unions, finance 
companies, and similar.
All businesses classified as Financial shall pay a flat annual assessment.
For Professional and Service businesses, the number of employees shall be determined based on Full 
Time Equivalent of 40 hours a week as reported on the initial business license application and updated 
thereafter during the annual business license renewal.
Footnote
1 - $3,050 + $14.00 per employee over 75 employees for Zone 1 
2- $1,525 + $10.00 per employee over 75 employees for Zone 2
3 - $2,300 + $10.00 per employee over 75 employees for Zone 1
4 - $1,150 + $7.00 per employee over 75 employees for Zone 2

DOWNTOWN SAN MATEO
ASSOCIATION SUMMARY 
The Downtown San Mateo Association (DSMA) continues
to play a pivotal role in fostering the resilience and vibrancy
of Downtown San Mateo. Since 1986, DSMA has managed
the Downtown Business Improvement District, supporting
merchants and nurturing community connections.
Downtown San Mateo is a regional hub for business and
leisure, attracting visitors from across the Bay Area with its
diverse mix of businesses, notably its renowned dining
scene, and its strategic location in Silicon Valley.
Under the leadership of Executive Director Mackenzie
Jakoubek, DSMA has prioritized collaboration and
community engagement as key strategies for activating
Downtown San Mateo. Through partnerships with local
organizations and active participation in and coordination
of community events, DSMA has successfully enhanced
Downtown's appeal and visibility. Looking ahead to Fiscal
Year 2024-25, DSMA remains committed to building upon
its achievements, with a focus on expanding marketing
and activation efforts, bolstering civic partnerships, and
enhancing Downtown's cleanliness and attractiveness.
With a projected budget reflecting strategic resource
allocation and continued support from its dedicated Board
of Directors, DSMA is poised to further elevate Downtown
San Mateo as a vibrant destination for residents, visitors,
and businesses alike.
$96k used for
Downtown clean-up
efforts
JUNE 2024
310 of 671

--- page 311 ---
In the heart of the Peninsula, Downtown San Mateo stands as a testament to resilience,
diversity, and evolution– a dynamic hub where commerce and community connect. At its
core is the Downtown San Mateo Association (DSMA), a key player in shaping the district's
vibrancy.
Each year since 1986, the DSMA has been designated by City Council to manage the
Downtown Business Improvement District. We are honored to be tasked with supporting the
merchants of Downtown San Mateo and bringing together the community who loves this
downtown district. 
FY 2023-24 ANNUAL REPORT
INTRODUCTION
JUNE 2024
2
311 of 671

--- page 312 ---
Thanks to its regionally central location,
Downtown San Mateo is a popular place to
meet for both business and leisure purposes.
Visitors come from San Francisco, Santa
Clara County, the East Bay, and, most often,
nearby Peninsula cities. Downtown is known
for its abundance and variety of restaurants,
which make up more than 20% of district
businesses. It is also an important node in the
Silicon Valley ecosystem, hosting start-ups
and tech-based companies of all sizes. The
presence of Mills Health Center means that
Downtown also includes many medical
professionals and other healthcare uses.
While there are traditional retailers in the
district, the storefronts on most blocks are
dominated by food and drink purveyors,
along with services such as salons, fitness
studios, and banks. Several body shops and
automotive businesses can be found east of
the railroad tracks.
In 2024, COVID-19 is behind us, but remote
work remains prevalent. The hybrid work
model continues to shape the reality of the
Bay Area, and the traditional Monday
through Friday, 9-5 office routine has
become less common. However, due to the
balance between housing and office spaces
in Downtown, workers and residents can be
observed working in coffee shops and
restaurants throughout the day. The hybrid
work setup also facilitates flexibility for
fitness studios, salons, and other personal
services to cater to clients at various times,
FY 2023-24 ANNUAL REPORT
DOWNTOWN SAN
MATEO OVERVIEW
especially during lunch hours. Weekends
are consistently busy, particularly in the
evenings, with residents and visitors
enjoying Downtown’s varied offerings.
Like all downtown areas, San Mateo has
encountered challenges in the past year.
Some small businesses continue to feel the
financial setbacks from COVID, impacting
their operations today. Rising costs due to
inflation have further squeezed their bottom
lines. Additionally, recent layoffs in Silicon
Valley have brought about uncertainty,
leading to reduced personal spending,
particularly on dining out, personal services,
and consumer goods.
Downtown San Mateo grapples with
common challenges for all business
districts. Downtown patrons voice
dissatisfaction with congested traffic,
insufficient parking availability, and unclean
surroundings. While businesses voice
discontentment around rising costs,
inconsistent foot traffic, difficulty finding
help, and other day-to-day obstacles.
Despite these obstacles, the overall
Downtown outlook from businesses and
visitors is positive. New businesses continue
to open up in our district, often citing safety,
cleanliness, and lively atmosphere as the
main reasons for selecting Downtown San
Mateo. Visitors have noted the positive
energy of Downtown, especially with the
increase in special events over the past
year. 
JUNE 2024
3
312 of 671

--- page 313 ---
In November 2022, the City Council
approved a modification to the assessments
for Downtown's business improvement
district (BID). This decision was necessary,
as the assessments had not received an
increase since 2009, and the fee structure
remained largely unchanged since the BID's
establishment in 1986. The updated
assessments aim for equity, ensuring that all
businesses that benefit from the BID
contribute their fair share to keep the
Downtown clean, safe, and lively.
Additionally, the new assessments better
reflect the diverse mix of businesses in
Downtown, spanning retail, professional
services, technology, and personal services.
Under the previous assessments, DSMA was
unable to hire full-time staff or expand
operations. The approval of the assessments
and annual levy are essential for the DSMA's
success. It allows DSMA to partner with the
City on the mission of fostering an active and
sustainable Downtown for businesses,
visitors, and residents.
In July 2023, DSMA hired Executive Director
Mackenzie Jakoubek. With more than 15
years of experience in nonprofit
management, customer service, and event
planning, Mackenzie hit the ground running,
adding to the incredible efforts over the past
five years from DSMA’s all-volunteer “army”
of Board Members.
FY 2023-24 ANNUAL REPORT
DSMA OVERVIEW
Mackenzie’s first goal was to connect with
and learn from Downtown merchants,
DSMA Board members past and present,
City of San Mateo staff and leadership,
involved residents, and her counterparts at
other BIDs. She quickly became the “face” of
DSMA and brought attention and
enthusiasm to Downtown San Mateo. At the
same time, she tackled administrative tasks
that had not been addressed since 2017,
when the last Director was in place. 
One important theme for Mackenzie in her
first year was collaboration. By collaborating
with like-minded entities, the DSMA aims to
achieve more, collectively, recognizing that
a combined effort yields greater results for
Downtown San Mateo. Through
partnerships with San Mateo Parks &
Recreation, San Mateo’s City Manager’s
Office, Self-Help for the Elderly, San Mateo
Age-Friendly Coalition, B Street Boosters,
and San Mateo Area Chamber of
Commerce, plus many BID members, DSMA
has been able to achieve far more than if
working alone.

DSMA activities for the past several years have addressed three focus areas: Community
Engagement, BID Member Engagement, and Civic Partnership.
FY 2023-24 ANNUAL REPORT
DSMA FOCUS AREAS
COMMUNITY
ENGAGEMENT
Community engagement focuses on coordinating events and marketing. The goal is to drive
traffic Downtown and connect the community with our wonderful and diverse businesses. The
primary audiences for community engagement are Downtown workers, San Mateo and
Peninsula residents, and visitors to local hotels and events.
COMMUNITY EVENTS 
(*INDICATES POP UP PERMIT EVENT)
North B Fiesta (Aug 27): This annual
event is a way for the merchants of North
B Street to thank their loyal customers
and share arts and culture from the many
Latin and Hispanic groups represented
on the block. DSMA was a sponsor and
booth participant.
September Nights on B (Sep 7 & 14):
Live music event on the Pedestrian Mall
hosted by the City of San Mateo with
support from the DSMA.

COMMUNITY EVENTS CONT’D
San Mateo Fall Fest (Sep 30): This day-
long event offered a variety of live music,
entertainment, local arts, and family-
friendly activities on B Street and beyond,
presented by the San Mateo Area
Chamber of Commerce in conjunction
with California Artists. DSMA was a
sponsor and assisted the Chamber with
coordination and fundraising. 
BOOs on B Street & Trick or Treating
(Oct 28): A close collaboration between
San Mateo’s Parks & Recreation
Department and DSMA, BOOs on B
Street welcomed hundreds of kids to the
B Street pedestrian mall to enjoy carnival
games, face painting, a bounce house,
and Halloween activities. DSMA
coordinated with 31 Downtown
merchants who provided candy to
adorable trick-or-treaters. Many
attendees also participated in our
Halloween scavenger hunt that led them
through the entire Downtown.
Las Posadas* (Dec 3): The merchants of
North B Street welcomed their loyal
customers and Holiday Train visitors to
their storefronts with warm cider, gift
bags, and other treats. Unidos en Cristo
sang Spanish-language Christmas carols
throughout the block. The event was
coordinated by the North B Merchants
with DSMA support.
Pre-Holiday Train Entertainment*
(Dec 3): Caltrain’s beloved annual
Holiday Train stopped at the San Mateo
station the evening of Dec 3rd. To
entertain the large crowd (mostly
families with small children) as they
gathered, DSMA coordinated a
demonstration from Core Taekwondo
and a festive holiday music performance
from San Mateo High School’s
Symphonic Band.

DSMA uses social media, email newsletters, and DSMA.org to connect with the community. 
Contracted a travel photographer to get updated snapshots that better represent our lively
Downtown for social media, website, and other marketing use
Announce DSMA events, Downtown happenings, storefront openings & anniversaries, sales
and promotions, City of San Mateo announcements, and Downtown businesses in the news
Project the Downtown “brand” by sharing snapshots of Downtown (both current and
nostalgic), sharing videos and photos after Downtown events occur, highlighting district
businesses, and engaging with other San Mateo-related accounts to keep Downtown San
Mateo in the conversation
Bring awareness and excitement to campaigns such as Shop Small to Win Big and the
Downtown San Mateo Restaurant Guide
Since July 2023, DSMA’s Facebook reach has increased by 160% and we have gained 395 new
followers. Our Instagram reach has increased by more than 2,000% and gained over 1,000 new
followers. The number of Downtown San Mateo Association Community News email subscribers
has increased by 55%.

FY 2023-24 ANNUAL REPORT
BUDGET
FY 2023-24 saw a significant increase in revenue due to the BID assessment modification
approved by City Council. We anticipate FY 2024-25 revenue will remain the same, or increase
slightly due to new businesses opening in the district. We have also added a line for potential
sponsorship revenue. Sponsoring activities Downtown is a way for businesses and community
organizations to show their whole-hearted support for Downtown San Mateo activation. 
Spending in FY 2023-24 was slightly conservative as the Board wanted to see the full impact of
the assessment change. For FY 2024-25, DSMA will have a better understanding of assessment
revenue and can use the expense budget more effectively. 
The expense highlights are:
Programmatic
Community events
Banners
Civic Partnership
Downtown Cleaning Porter
Marketing
Advertising and Promotion
Social Media contractor
Photographer
Payroll & Professional Services
Executive Director
Internship
Administration
A small contingency fund for unplanned expenses
The DSMA projects a cash balance of $211.0K at the start of FY 2024-25 and projects a cash
balance of approximately $211.0K at the end of the budgeted FY 2023-24.
DSMA is redirecting the savings from sharing an office with the Downtown Coordinator towards
programs that support our mission. While DSMA doesn’t utilize any city equipment, the shared
space fosters a closer relationship between DSMA, the City, and Downtown businesses. We
would like to express our sincere gratitude to the City for providing the shared space and for its
ongoing support and partnership.
ESTIMATED INCOME AND
EXPENSES FOR FY 2024-25

--- page 340 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  24
Section Name: PUBLIC HEARING
Account Number: 25-3112
File ID: 24-8827
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
June 17, 2024 
SUBJECT:
By-Right Housing Opportunity Overlay District – Zoning Code and Map Amendments – Ordinance Introduction
RECOMMENDATION:
Introduce an Ordinance amending San Mateo Municipal Code Title 27 Zoning, to add Chapter 27.94, Housing 
Opportunities Overlay District (H), which would allow certain types of housing projects by-right on specified parcels, and 
amending the San Mateo Zoning Map to establish the “H” overlay on 39 parcels; and introduce a separate ordinance 
reclassifying the zoning for 200 S. Fremont Street from R4-D to R4-D/H; and find that the ordinances are exempt from 
further environmental review pursuant to California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Section 15183 (Projects Consistent 
With A Community Plan, General Plan, or Zoning).
BACKGROUND:
Since 1969, California has required that all local governments (cities and counties) adequately plan to meet the housing 
needs of their community. Local governments meet this requirement by adopting housing elements, which are updated 
every 8 years. The housing element is a "chapter” of a city’s General Plan, which is also required by State law. However, it 
is the only element of the General Plan subject to State requirements for content, and the State Department of Housing 
and Community Development (HCD) makes a determination regarding whether it is consistent with state law. State 
funding programs for transportation, infrastructure, and housing often require or consider a local jurisdiction’s compliance 
with housing element law and whether HCD has approved the housing element. In addition, non-compliance with housing 
element requirements could potentially affect a local jurisdiction’s ability to impose local zoning and land use controls over 
housing development and receive certain types of State funding and grants.
On May 20, 2024, the City Council adopted San Mateo’s 2023-2031 Housing Element to affirmatively further fair housing 
and accommodate the City’s 7,015-unit Regional Housing Needs Allocation (RHNA) for the Sixth Housing Element Cycle 
(2023-2031). The adopted Housing Element was submitted to HCD on May 24, 2024. 
The Housing Element identifies how the City can accommodate its RHNA of 7,015 new units over the next eight years, and 
establishes goals, policies, and programs to help address the City’s current and future housing needs. Under Goal 1 – 
Production, the City commits to facilitate and support the production of new housing at all income levels, but especially 
affordable housing. Twenty policies with 52 implementing actions have been identified to support this goal. 
One of the identified policies under the Production goal, Policy H 1.10, commits the City to establishing a by-right housing 
designation for housing opportunity sites identified in a prior housing element, pursuant to Government Code 65583.2(c). 
Specifically, the implementation action within this policy requires the City to amend the Zoning Code and establish a by-
right zoning designation for housing development projects in which at least 20 percent of the units proposed are 
affordable to lower income households on these reused sites. Zoning for these sites must allow for at least 30 units per 
340 of 671

--- page 341 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
acre to comply with the State law. These amendments need to be adopted to ensure that HCD can certify the City’s 
Housing Element. The adoption of the by-right ordinance was delayed beyond the statutory deadline of January 31, 2024, 
due to General Plan and Housing Element adoption hearings in early 2024. The City advised HCD of this delay and 
coordinated with HCD on the timing of the Housing Element and by-right ordinance. City staff shared a draft of the 
Ordinance with HCD for review and input on May 3, 2024, and HCD provided preliminary approval on May 6, 2024. 
DISCUSSION: 
To implement the General Plan and Housing Element policies, State law requires that the City review and update the 
Zoning Code to ensure that the review processes and requirements are compatible with the objectives, policies, and 
programs specified in the General Plan. San Mateo Municipal Code section 27.060.040(c) requires the Planning 
Commission to review amendments to the Zoning Code and make a recommendation to the City Council. 
The proposed zoning code amendments (Attachments 1 and 2) would create a new chapter in the Zoning Code, namely 
Chapter 27.94, Housing Opportunities Overlay District (H) (“Ordinance”). The Ordinance would create eligibility 
parameters and review processes for housing development projects that meet the by-right requirements of Government 
Code 65583.2(c) and Policy 1.10 of the Housing Element. The Ordinance also amends the Zoning Map to create an H 
Overlay District on 40 identified parcels that were listed in prior housing elements as opportunity sites and thus qualify for 
this streamlined review process through the establishment of this overlay district. These sites include 35 nonvacant sites 
that were identified in the prior housing element and five (5) vacant sites that have been included in two or more 
consecutive housing elements and were not approved for development. 
One Council member’s real property interest is within 500 feet of one of the eligible parcels, namely 200 S. Fremont.  Staff 
therefore prepared a separate ordinance to rezone this property.  The entire Council may first vote on the zoning text 
changes and overlay district, except for the parcel at 200 S. Fremont.  Then, the rest of the Council may vote on rezoning 
the subject parcel while one Council member is recused.  See Attachment 3 for a Parcel Disposition Table and a Map of the 
subject sites. 
The Ordinance would also allow housing development projects that propose 20% of the units as affordable to lower 
income households to be reviewed and approved without discretionary review or environmental review under the 
California Environmental Quality Act, pursuant to Government Code Section 65583.2(i). Qualifying housing projects 
compliant with the City’s Municipal Code and Objective Design Standards (approved by the Council on November 20, 
2023) would be subject to Zoning Administrator approval without discretionary review or public hearings. All applicable 
standard conditions of approval may still be imposed on these projects. Since this approval would be ministerial, the 
Zoning Administrator decision for such projects would be final, and thus could not be appealed to the Planning 
Commission or City Council. This new process would allow further streamlining during the review process for all by-right 
housing projects proposed at these sites. 
Collectively, if developed at 30 units per acre, the 40 identified sites would result in 1,007 housing units, out of which 220 
units would be affordable to the lower income households. The sites may be developed at higher or lower densities as 
allowed by the underlying base zoning. All requirements and standards associated with the underlying base zoning would 
remain unchanged, and existing permitted uses would not be affected by the creation of the H Overlay District. 
The zoning designation for by-right residential uses allowing 30 dwelling units per acre is within the density limits of the 
land use designations of the San Mateo General Plan 2040, and as set by Measure Y (the City’s voter approved density 
limits). The proposed code amendments will implement Policy H 1.10 of the Housing Element; therefore, the proposed 
Ordinance is consistent with the City’s adopted General Plan. 
Planning Commission Recommendation
The Planning Commission reviewed the proposed amendments on May 28, 2024 , and recommended that Council adopt 
the proposed amendments to the zoning code and the zoning map to establish the H Overlay District. The Planning 
Commission also included an advisory recommendation that Council consider allowing by-right housing projects at the 
City’s existing inclusionary requirement (15% of base density) when prevailing wages are paid. Staff does not recommend 
341 of 671

--- page 342 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
incorporating the Planning Commission’s advisory recommendation into the Ordinance. The City’s adopted General Plan 
2040 includes policies and actions that support local hiring and address living wages issues. Moreover, the Ordinance is 
narrowly focused on creating a specific zoning designation to comply with State law. 
Next Steps 
Following the City Council public hearing to introduce the Ordinance, it will be brought back for adoption through a second 
reading at a subsequent City Council meeting and would become effective 30 days after adoption. The final adopted 
version of the Ordinance will be shared with HCD to facilitate certification of the City’s Housing Element. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Policy work and code updates to support Housing Element implementation are accounted for within the Community 
Development Department’s annual budget, with funding for this activity coming from the Advance Planning Fund.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The Environmental Impact Report (“EIR”) for the San Mateo General Plan 2040 analyzed new residential development of 
up to 19,764 new housing units and growth around the City through 2040.  On March 18, 2024, the City Council certified 
the EIR.  In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act (“CEQA”) Guidelines Section 15183, the Ordinance to 
establish a by-right residential overlay district and reclassify parcels with the overlay district (“project”) is exempt from 
further environmental review because the Project is consistent with the land use designation for the sites adopted as part 
of the General Plan 2040 for which an EIR was certified.  Also, the residential development density allowed by the 
Ordinance is consistent with the development density established by zoning or general plan policies.  
Applying Section 15183, there are no project specific effects that are peculiar to the project or the sites; there are no 
project specific impacts that the General Plan EIR failed to analyze as significant effects; there are no potentially significant 
off-site and cumulative impacts that the general plan EIR failed to evaluate; and there is no substantial new information 
which results in more severe impacts than anticipated by the General Plan EIR.  Furthermore, any future development of 
sites specified in these code amendments would not constitute a “project” for CEQA purposes due to their non-
discretionary nature, pursuant to Government Code Section 65583.2(i).  A detailed analysis in support of this CEQA 
determination is included as Attachment 4.
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met. A public hearing notice of the proposed code amendments, pursuant to 
Government Code Sections 65090 and 65091, was published in the San Mateo Daily Journal, and posted at three public 
locations within the City. Mailed notices were provided to the 40 property owners that would qualify for the new H 
Overlay District and to the Department’s general notification list; and information about this initiative was sent out 
through the Department’s e-newsletter. 
PUBLIC COMMENTS: 
Staff has not received any public comments as of the publication of the agenda report. Public comments submitted after 
agenda publication will be posted to this item on the City’s Public Meeting Portal as “Post Packet Public Comments.”

CITY OF SAN MATEO 
DRAFT ORDINANCE 
Adding Chapter 27.94, Housing (H) Opportunities Overlay District, to Title 27, Zoning, of the San 
Mateo Municipal Code and Amending the Zoning Map of the City of San Mateo to Establish the 
Housing Opportunities Overlay District (H)   
WHEREAS, the State Housing Element Law (Government Code Section 65580 et seq.) requires that 
the City Council adopt a Housing Element for the eight-year period of 2023-2031 to accommodate the 
City of San Mateo (“City”) regional housing need allocation (RHNA) of 7,015 housing units; and  
WHEREAS, on May 20, 2024, the City Council adopted a resolution to adopt the revised Housing 
Element of the General Plan for the period of 2023-2031 (Housing Element); and 
WHEREAS, Government Code Section 65583.2(c) requires nonvacant sites identified in a prior 
housing element and vacant sites that have been included in two or more consecutive planning periods 
that were not approved to develop a portion of the City’s assigned RHNA to be rezoned to allow for 
residential use by-right for housing developments in which at least 20 percent of the units are affordable 
to lower income households; and  
WHEREAS, pursuant to Government Code Section 65583.2(c), Policy H 1.10 of the 2023-2031 
Housing Element requires amending the Zoning Code to establish a by-right zoning designation for 
housing sites reused from prior Housing Elements for housing projects that propose a minimum of 20 
percent affordable units for lower income households and allows a minimum of 30 dwelling units per 
acre; and  
WHEREAS, the Housing Element has identified sites that can accommodate sufficient housing 
units to meet the City’s RHNA plus a buffer, and includes 35 nonvacant sites that were identified in the 
prior housing element and five (5) vacant sites that have been included in two or more consecutive 
planning periods and were not approved for development; and 
WHEREAS, the zoning designation for by-right residential uses allowing 30 dwelling units per 
acre is within the density limits of the land use designations of the San Mateo General Plan 2040; and 
WHEREAS, the Environmental Impact Report (EIR) for the San Mateo General Plan 2040 analyzed 
new residential development of up to 19,764 new housing units and growth around the City through 
2040; and  
WHEREAS, on March 18, 2024, at a duly noticed public hearing, the City Council certified the EIR 
for the San Mateo General Plan 2040, pursuant to the California Environmental Quality Act of 1970, 
together with related State implementation guidelines promulgated thereunder (CEQA); and 
WHEREAS, on May 28, 2024, the Planning Commission held a duly noticed public hearing, 
received all written and oral public comments, and recommended that the City Council approve the 
proposed ordinance establishing the Housing Opportunity Overlay District (H) and amending the zoning 
map to reclassify parcels on housing sites used in previous Housing Elements with the Housing 
Opportunity Overlay District (H). 
 
344 of 671

--- page 345 ---
NOW, THEREFORE, THE COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO ORDAINS AS FOLLOWS: 
 
SECTION 1. Legislative Findings.  At a duly noticed public hearing on _____________________, 2024, 
the City Council of the City of San Mateo reviewed and considered the accompanying staff report and 
attachments thereto, the exemption from environmental review, together with comments received 
during the public review process, and hereby finds and determines that the proposed ordinance is 
consistent with the adopted General Plan in that it supports the development of housing and achieves 
the General Plan’s stated goals and policies. 
SECTION 2. Chapter 27.94 is added to Title 27 of the San Mateo Municipal Code as follows: 
Chapter 27.94 Housing Opportunities Overlay District (H)   
27.94.005 Purpose 
The purpose of the Housing Opportunities Overlay District (H) is to establish by-right residential uses on 
sites identiﬁed to accommodate a portion of the housing need for lower income housing in the General 
Plan Housing Element, pursuant to Government Code Sections 65583.2(c) and (i).  This chapter 
establishes minimum development requirements and procedures for reviewing and approval of the by-
right residential development uses. 
 
27.94.010 By-Right Residential Use Eligibility 
 
The following housing development projects are eligible for by-right approval.  Housing development 
projects that are ineligible for by-right approval shall be subject to the requirements for residential 
development in the underlying zoning district.  
(a) Pursuant to Government Code Section 65583.2(c) and (h), housing development projects in 
which at least 20 percent of the units proposed are affordable to lower income households, as defined 
in Health and Safety Code Section 50079.5, and is a household whose income is equal to or less than 
eighty percent of the area median income, as published by the California Department of Housing and 
Community Development.  The housing development project shall be allowed at the density allowed in 
the underlying zoning district and shall be allowed a minimum density of at least 30 dwelling units per 
acre. A “housing development project” shall have the same meaning as defined in Government Code 
Section 65589.5(h)(2).   
27.94.020 By-Right Residential Use Requirements 
 
(a) Site plan and architectural review approval is required under Chapter 27.08, Rules of 
Procedure. The Zoning Administrator shall be the approval authority for projects proposed pursuant to 
this chapter that comply with the City’s Municipal Code and Objective Design Standards. The Zoning 
Administrator shall not exercise any discretion in the review process for such projects and their decision 
shall be final.     
 
(b) The site plan and architectural review shall not constitute a “project” for the purposes of 
Division 13 (commencing with Section 21000) of the Public Resources Code.  
 
345 of 671

--- page 346 ---
(c) Any subdivision of the site shall be subject to all laws, including, but not limited to Title 26 of 
the City of San Mateo Municipal Code (Subdivisions).  
 
(d) Notwithstanding Section 27.94.010, the following objective development standards shall 
apply to housing development projects built pursuant to this chapter: 
 
(1) Development standards set forth in the underlying zoning district. 
 
(2) For a project within another overlay district, the development standards of the overlay 
district. 
 
(3) For development projects within an E2 Zoning District without an overlay district, the R4 
development standards. 
 
(4) For development projects within a Specific Plan or within the TOD zoning district, the 
applicable development standards of the Specific Plan or TOD zoning district. 
 
(5) Objective Design Standards for multi-family and residential mixed-use projects as 
adopted by City Council resolution. 
 
(6) Applicable standard conditions of approval. 
 
SECTION 3. Environmental Determination. In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act 
(CEQA) Guidelines Section 15183, the zoning amendment to establish a by-right residential overlay 
district and reclassify parcels with the overlay district (“project”) is exempt from further environmental 
review because the Project is consistent with the land use designation for the sites adopted as part of 
the General Plan 2040 for which an Environmental Impact Report (EIR) was certified by the City Council 
on March 18, 2024 and the allowed residential development density is also consistent with the 
development density established by zoning or general plan policies.  There are no project specific effects 
that are peculiar to the project or the sites; there are no project specific impacts that the General Plan 
Update EIR failed to analyze as significant effects; there are no potentially significant off-site and 
cumulative impacts that the general plan EIR failed to evaluate; and there is no substantial new 
information which results in more severe impacts than anticipated by the general plan EIR, all as further 
explained in the CEQA memorandum attached to the staff report. Furthermore, any future development 
of the sites pursuant to these code amendments would not constitute a “project” for purposes of 
Division 13 (commencing with Section 21000) of the Public Resources Code pursuant to Government 
Code Section 65583.2(i). 
SECTION 4. Zoning Map.  The Zoning Map of the City of San Mateo is hereby amended to add the 
Housing Opportunities Overlay District (H) on the 39 properties listed in Exhibit A and depicted in Exhibit 
B, attached hereto and incorporated by reference.  The Zoning Map shall be amended to reflect this 
amendment. 
346 of 671

--- page 347 ---
SECTION 5. Severability. In the event any section, clause or provision of this ordinance shall be 
determined invalid or unconstitutional, such section, clause or provision shall be deemed severable and 
all other sections or portions hereof shall remain in full force and effect. 
SECTION 6. Publication. This ordinance shall be published in summary in a newspaper of general 
circulation, posted in the City Clerk's Office, and posted on the City's website, all in accord with Section 
2.15 of the City Charter. 
SECTION 7. Legislative History And Effective Date. This ordinance was introduced on Clerk to complete., 
and adopted on Clerk to complete., and shall be effective 30 days after its adoption.

CITY OF SAN MATEO 
DRAFT ORDINANCE 
Amending the Zoning Map of the City of San Mateo to Rezone the Site at 200 S. Fremont St. (APN 033-
163-160) from R-D to R-D/H
WHEREAS, the State Housing Element Law (Government Code Section 65580 et seq.) requires that 
the City Council adopt a Housing Element for the eight-year period of 2023-2031 to accommodate the 
City of San Mateo (“City”) regional housing need allocation (RHNA) of 7,015 housing units; and  
WHEREAS, on May 20, 2024, the City Council adopted a resolution to adopt the revised Housing 
Element of the General Plan for the period of 2023-2031 (Housing Element); and 
WHEREAS, Government Code Section 65583.2(c) requires nonvacant sites identified in a prior 
housing element and vacant sites that have been included in two or more consecutive planning periods 
that were not approved to develop a portion of the City’s assigned RHNA to be rezoned to allow for 
residential use by-right for housing developments in which at least 20 percent of the units are affordable 
to lower income households; and  
WHEREAS, pursuant to Government Code Section 65583.2(c), Policy H 1.10 of the 2023-2031 
Housing Element requires amending the Zoning Code to establish a by-right zoning designation for 
housing sites reused from prior Housing Elements for housing projects that propose a minimum of 20 
percent affordable units for lower income households and allows a minimum of 30 dwelling units per 
acre; and  
WHEREAS, the Housing Element has identified sites that can accommodate sufficient housing 
units to meet the City’s RHNA plus a buffer, and included the site at 200 S. Fremont St. 35 which is a 
vacant site that was included in two or more consecutive planning periods; and 
WHEREAS, the existing zoning designation of 200 S. Fremont St. is in a Multiple Family Dwellings 
For Downtown (Medium High Density) zoning district; and 
WHEREAS, 200 S. Fremont St. is in the Residential Medium II land use designation of the San 
Mateo General Plan 2040 which allows up to 50 dwelling units per acre (pursuant to Measure Y 
limitations).  The zoning designation for by-right residential uses allowing 30 dwelling units per acre is 
within the density limits; and 
WHEREAS, the Environmental Impact Report (EIR) for the San Mateo General Plan 2040 analyzed 
new residential development of up to 19,764 new housing units and growth around the City through 
2040; and  
WHEREAS, on March 18, 2024, at a duly noticed public hearing, the City Council certified the EIR 
for the San Mateo General Plan 2040, pursuant to the California Environmental Quality Act of 1970, 
together with related State implementation guidelines promulgated thereunder (CEQA); and 
WHEREAS, on May 28, 2024, the Planning Commission held a duly noticed public hearing, 
received all written and oral public comments, and recommended that the City Council approve the 
proposed ordinance establishing the Housing Opportunity Overlay District (H) and amending the zoning 
353 of 671

--- page 354 ---
map to reclassify parcels on housing sites used in previous Housing Elements with the Housing 
Opportunity Overlay District (H). 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO ORDAINS AS FOLLOWS: 
SECTION 1. Legislative Findings.  At a duly noticed public hearing on _____________________, 2024, 
the City Council of the City of San Mateo reviewed and considered the accompanying staff report and 
attachments thereto, the exemption from environmental review, together with comments received 
during the public review process, and hereby finds and determines that the proposed ordinance is 
consistent with the adopted General Plan in that it supports the development of housing and achieves 
the General Plan’s stated goals and policies. 
SECTION 2. Environmental Determination. In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act 
(CEQA) Guidelines Section 15183, the zoning amendment to reclassify 200 S Fremont St. with the 
overlay district (“project”) is exempt from further environmental review because the project is 
consistent with the Residential Medium II land use designation adopted as part of the General Plan 2040 
for which an Environmental Impact Report (EIR) was certified by the City Council on March 18, 2024 and 
the allowed residential development density is also consistent with the development density established 
by zoning or general plan policies.  There are no project specific effects that are peculiar to the project; 
there are no project specific impacts that the General Plan Update EIR failed to analyze as significant 
effects; there are no potentially significant off-site and cumulative impacts that the general plan EIR 
failed to evaluate; and there is no substantial new information which results in more severe impacts 
than anticipated by the general plan EIR, all as further explained in the CEQA memorandum attached to 
the staff report. Furthermore, any future development of the site pursuant to these code amendments 
would not constitute a “project” for purposes of Division 13 (commencing with Section 21000) of the 
Public Resources Code pursuant to Government Code Section 65583.2(i). 
SECTION 3. Zoning Map.  The Zoning Map of the City of San Mateo is hereby amended to rezone the 
property generally known as 200 S. Fremont St. (APN 033-163-160) from R4-D to R4-D/H as depicted in 
Exhibit A, attached hereto and incorporated by reference.  The Zoning Map shall be amended to reflect 
this amendment.  
SECTION 4. Severability. In the event any section, clause or provision of this ordinance shall be 
determined invalid or unconstitutional, such section, clause or provision shall be deemed severable and 
all other sections or portions hereof shall remain in full force and effect. 
SECTION 5. Publication. This ordinance shall be published in summary in a newspaper of general 
circulation, posted in the City Clerk's Office, and posted on the City's website, all in accord with Section 
2.15 of the City Charter. 
SECTION 6. Legislative History And Effective Date. This ordinance was introduced on Clerk to complete., 
and adopted on Clerk to complete., and shall be effective 30 days after its adoption.

Building Division (650) 522-7172 · Code Enforcement Division (650) 522-7150
Housing Division (650) 522-7220 · Planning Division (650) 522-7212 · General (650) 522-7200 
                                                              
California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Exemption Evaluation 
Project Name: Housing Opportunities Overlay District Ordinance Amendments
Project Location: 40 Parcels Citywide
Lead Agency: City of San Mateo – Community Development Department
Date: May 22, 2024
Purpose
The purpose of this document is to evaluate the required findings under California Environmental 
Quality Act (CEQA) Guidelines Section 15183 (Projects Consistent with a Community Plan or Zoning) 
for purposes of assessing environmental impacts associated with proposed zoning ordinance and 
map amendments in order to establish a Housing Opportunities Overlay District, consistent with 
State law and City’s 2023-2031 Housing Element Policy 1.10.  Pursuant to CEQA Statute and 
Guidelines Section 15183, projects which are consistent with the development density established by 
existing zoning, community plan, or general plan policies for which an Environmental Impact Report 
(EIR) was certified shall not require additional environmental review, except as might be necessary to 
examine whether there are project-specific significant effects which are peculiar to the project or its 
site. This streamlines the review of such projects and reduces the need to prepare repetitive 
environmental studies.
Background
San Mateo General Plan 2040 EIR
As part of the City’s comprehensive general plan update process, an EIR was prepared in compliance 
with the CEQA Statute and Guidelines (California Code of Regulations §15000 et. seq.) and the 
regulations and policies of the City for the San Mateo General Plan 2040. On March 18, 2024, the City 
Council certified the Final EIR for the San Mateo General Plan 2040 and adopted the General Plan 
2040.  One of the primary purposes of General Plan 2040 is to plan for the growth and conservation 
of the City of San Mateo over a 20-year horizon. General Plan 2040 identified the location and 
allowed density and intensity of San Mateo’s land uses including housing and identified sufficient 
residential land to meet the current and future housing needs for people at all income levels. The 
General Plan 2040 EIR included buildout projections that included growth associated with current 
development projects in the planning entitlement pipeline and development of sites in the City’s 
Housing Element 2023-2031 Housing Sites Inventory. According to the buildout projections, the 
General Plan EIR accounted for a total of 21,410 housing units.
CEQA provides that decision-makers should not approve a project as proposed if there are feasible
alternatives or feasible mitigation measures that would substantially lessen the significant impacts of
COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT DEPARTMENT
                                            330 West 20th Avenue
                                             San Mateo, CA 94403
                               www.cityofsanmateo.org
360 of 671

--- page 361 ---
Building Division (650) 522-7172 · Code Enforcement Division (650) 522-7150
Housing Division (650) 522-7220 · Planning Division (650) 522-7212 · General (650) 522-7200 
the project (Public Resources Code §21002). In certifying the General Plan EIR the City adopted a 
statement of overriding considerations, for certain significant and unavoidable impacts.  Impacts 
related to Air Quality, Noise, and Wildfire Risks were identified as significant and unavoidable in the 
Final EIR and no feasible mitigation or project alternative were identified or available to reduce these 
impacts at a programmatic level to a level of less than significance:
Proposed Project
The proposed project includes zoning ordinance and map amendments in order to establish a 
Housing Opportunities Overlay District, consistent with State law and City’s 2023-2031 Housing 
Element Policy 1.10. Policy H 1.10, commits the City to establishing a by-right housing designation 
(i.e., nondiscretionary review and approval) for prior housing opportunity sites identified in a prior 
housing element, pursuant to Government Code 65583.2(c). Specifically, the implementation action 
within this policy requires the City to amend the Zoning Code and establish a by-right zoning 
designation for housing development project in which at least 20 percent of the units proposed are 
affordable to lower income households on these reused sites and zoning for these sites must allow at 
least 30 units per acre to meet default density requirements for lower-income households comply 
with the State law.  
To implement the Housing Element Policy and compliance with Government Code 65583.2(c), the 
City is proposing to create a new chapter to amend Title 27, Zoning, of the San Mateo Municipal 
Code to establish the Housing Opportunities Overlay District (H) and to amend the Zoning Map of the 
City of San Mateo to reclassify 40 parcels that were previously identified as housing opportunity 
sites, with the “H” designation to allow by-right housing development. In addition to the zoning 
designation requiring the housing development provide for a minimum of 20 percent of the units for 
lower income households and allowing a density of at least 30 dwelling units per acre, Government 
Code 65583.2(i) specifies that the local government cannot apply any discretion in their review and 
the review would not constitute a “project” for purposes of the California Environmental Quality Act 
(Division 13 (commencing with Section 21000)) of the Public Resources Code.
Summary of CEQA Findings
To be eligible for exemption under CEQA Guideline Section 15183, a proposed project must be 
consistent with the development density established by existing zoning, community plan, or general 
plan policies for which an EIR was certified. The proposed zoning code and map amendments, to 
create the Housing Opportunity Overlay District (H), is consistent with the density established by the 
San Mateo General Plan 2040 and the related certified EIR because it will allow at least 30 dwelling 
units per acre which is lower than the maximum density allowed per the General Plan land use 
designation (see Attachment 2 of the agenda report).  
Section 15183 also requires that a city in approving a project meeting the requirements of this 
section of the CEQA Guidelines can limit its examination of environmental effects to the following 
items:
1. Are peculiar to the project or the parcel on which the project would be located.
Findings: The San Mateo General Plan 2040 identified the location and allowed density and intensity 
of San Mateo’s land uses including housing and identified sufficient residential land to meet the 
current and future housing needs for people at all income levels.  The General Plan EIR included 
361 of 671

--- page 362 ---
Building Division (650) 522-7172 · Code Enforcement Division (650) 522-7150
Housing Division (650) 522-7220 · Planning Division (650) 522-7212 · General (650) 522-7200 
buildout projections of 21,410 housing units that included growth associated with current 
development projects in the planning entitlement pipeline and development of sites in the City’s 
2023-2031 Housing Sites Inventory.
The zoning amendment to create the Housing Opportunity Overlay District that implements the by-
right housing designations does not create any peculiar environmental effects because the land use 
designations adopted in the General Plan already account for residential uses and the minimum 
allowed density required in the proposed housing opportunities overlay district (30 dwelling units per 
acre)  is lower than the maximum density allowed per the General Plan land use designation for 
individual parcels where the overlay district is being established (see attached table).
The parcels that the overlay district will be applied to were individually analyzed for specific 
environmental constraints when they were added to the City’s sites inventory and no unusual 
circumstances were identified that would preclude their addition on the sites inventory. None of the 
40 sites are located within the wildfire hazard areas of the City. 
In addition, pursuant to Government Code Section 65583.2(i), by-right housing development projects 
within the adopted Housing Opportunity Overlay District shall not constitute a “project” for purposes 
of the California Environmental Quality Act (Division 13 (commencing with Section 21000) of the 
Public Resources Code.  Therefore, no further environmental review will be required.  However, the 
proposed housing opportunities overlay district does allow the City to apply standard conditions of 
approval which the City will use to address specific potential environmental impacts such as air 
quality and noise impacts as identified in the General Plan EIR.
2. Were not analyzed as significant effects in a prior EIR on the zoning action, general plan,
or community plan, with which the project is consistent.
Findings: General Plan 2040 identified the location and allowed density and intensity of San Mateo’s 
land uses including housing and identified sufficient residential land to meet the current and future 
housing needs for people at all income levels. The General Plan EIR included buildout projections of 
21,410 housing units that included growth associated with current development projects in the 
planning entitlement pipeline and development of sites in the City’s 2023-2031 Housing Sites 
Inventory.
The zoning amendment to create the Housing Opportunity Overlay District that implements the by-
right housing designations does not create any changes to the uses or residential densities already 
allowed in the land use designations adopted in the General Plan. The proposed zoning amendment 
does not create any specific impacts not previously reviewed by the General Plan 2040 EIR.
While the parcels that the overlay district will be applied to have not been individually analyzed for 
specific environmental impacts, pursuant to Government Code Section 65583.2(i), by-right housing 
development projects within the adopted Housing Opportunity Overlay District shall not constitute a 
“project” for purposes of the California Environmental Quality Act (Division 13 (commencing with 
Section 21000) of the Public Resources Code. Therefore, no further environmental review will be 
required.  
3. Are potentially significant off-site impacts and cumulative impacts which were not
discussed in the prior EIR prepared for the general plan, community plan or zoning action.
362 of 671

--- page 363 ---
Building Division (650) 522-7172 · Code Enforcement Division (650) 522-7150
Housing Division (650) 522-7220 · Planning Division (650) 522-7212 · General (650) 522-7200 
Findings: 
The zoning amendment to create the Housing Opportunity Overlay District that implements the by-
right housing designations does not create any new significant off-site impacts or cumulative impacts 
which were not discussed in the General Plan EIR.  General Plan 2040 identified the location and 
allowed density and intensity of San Mateo’s land uses including housing and identified sufficient 
residential land to meet the current and future housing needs for people at all income levels.  The 
General Plan EIR included buildout projections of 21,410 housing units that included growth 
associated with current development projects in the planning entitlement pipeline and development 
of sites in the City’s 2023-2031 Housing Sites Inventory. The density and intensity of the land uses 
associated with the Housing Opportunity Overlay District is consistent with the General Plan land use 
designations, therefore, already considered in cumulative impacts.
4. Are previously identified significant effects which, as a result of substantial new
information which was not known at the time the EIR was certified, are determined to have
a more severe adverse impact than discussed in the prior EIR.
Findings: The certified General Plan 2040 Final EIR found significant effects including impacts related 
to air quality, noise, and wildland fire impacts (see Background section above for details).  The Final 
EIR was certified by the City Council on March 18, 2024.  There is no new substantial information 
which was not known at the time the EIR was certified that would create a more severe adverse 
impact than was discussed in the EIR.

MEETING DATE:

SUBJECT:
1528 S. El Camino Real - Zoning Map Amendment and Modifications to an Existing Mixed-Use Building (PA-2023-057) 
RECOMMENDATION:
Introduce an Ordinance to approve a Zoning Map Amendment to rezone a portion of the site at 1528 El Camino Real from 
R3 to C2-1/R4; and adopt a Resolution to approve the Site Plan and Architectural Review (SPAR) for interior changes and 
modifications to the conditions of approval for an existing mixed-use building to replace ten (10) live-work units with ten 
(10) residential units; and determine the project is exempt from the California Environmental Quality Act pursuant to 
Section 15183, based on the Findings for Approval and subject to the Conditions of Approval.
BACKGROUND:
In 1998, the City Council approved a new four-story, 60,790 square-foot, mixed-use building with commercial and office 
uses, and ten (10) live-work units, at 1528 S. El Camino Real. The project included a parcel map to merge three parcels 
separately zoned as R-3 and C2-1/R4, which resulted in a parcel with split zoning. Condition No. 69 imposed restrictions on 
the live-work units that the live-work units “shall be considered a residential occupancy” and the residential portion shall 
be no less than 540 square feet or 25% of the unit floor area, whichever is larger. In addition, the project received height 
and intensity bonuses to exceed the maximum allowable height and floor area ratio (FAR) of the base zoning district, given 
the mixed-use project included a residential component and provided amenities beyond the city requirements. 
The property owner reported that since the building has been occupied, these live-work units have been challenging to 
occupy for their intended use. In 2020, a code enforcement case documented that these live-work units did not include a 
residential component and were instead used solely as office spaces. To correct these on-site violations, the Applicant 
seeks to modify the building to convert the 10 live-work units to  10 residential units. No expansion is proposed. The 
zoning map amendment allows for reconfiguration of the building’s interior space.
The site is currently split-zoned, with the front portion along El Camino Real zoned C2-1/R4 (Regional/Community 
Commercial with Residential Overlay District - Mixed Use), and the rear portion along Jasmine Street zoned R3 (Multiple 
Family Dwellings-Medium Density). The existing building straddles both zoning designations; the use is office uses on the 
ground floor within the R3 zone. The zoning map amendment would redesignate the rear of the lot from R3 to C2-1/R4 to 
create one zoning designation for the whole site and allow for the entire mixed-use building to contain commercial, office, 
and residential uses. 
The Planning Commission reviewed the project application and unanimously recommended that the City Council approve 
the zoning map amendment and Site Plan and Architectural review (SPAR). The Draft Ordinance for the zoning map 
amendment is included in Attachment 1 and the Draft Resolution for the SPAR is included in Attachment 2.
Project Description
364 of 671

--- page 365 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
The proposed project includes a zoning map amendment, interior improvements to establish ten (10) new residential units 
within the building, and modifications to the conditions of approval to remove the live-work requirements. The building, as 
originally approved, included ten (10) one-bedroom live-work units for a total of approximately 26,609 square feet, of 
which 5,400 square feet was required to be for the residential portion of each unit. The proposed modification would 
create ten residential units (four studios, five one-bedrooms, and one two-bedroom apartments) ranging in size from 667 
to 1,152 square feet, totaling approximately 9,250 square feet within the existing building. The proposed project plans are 
included as Attachment 3 and the original plans approved in 1997 are included as Attachment 4.
The project site is located on an irregularly shaped parcel totaling 42,003 square feet (0.96 acres). The site has two street 
frontages and is bounded by S. El Camino Real to the east, Jasmine Street to the west, commercial and residential buildings 
to the north, and commercial buildings to the south. The site is designated Mixed-Use Medium II in the City’s General Plan 
2040 Land Use Plan. 
The existing four-story mixed-use building currently consists of professional and medical offices, a dance studio, a yoga 
studio, a massage facility, and a below-grade garage and surface parking with 112 parking spaces in total. The site is 
approximately one-half mile from the Hayward Park Caltrain Station. A location map showing the project site, its vicinity, 
and adjacent zoning designations is included as Attachment 5.
APPLICABLE CODE AND POLICY REVIEW:
General Plan and Zoning Map Amendment
The General Plan 2040 Land Use Map (Attachment 6) designates the project site as Mixed-Use Medium II. The Mixed-Use 
Medium II land use designation was established to provide for medium-density mixed-use buildings that provide a variety 
of commercial, office, and/or residential uses within the same site or building. 
The current R3 zoning at the rear of the property does not allow for standard office or commercial zoning, and therefore is 
not consistent with the General Plan 2040 land use designation. However, the requested C2-1/R4 zone would allow for the 
proposed project, as the C2-1 zone supports a broad range of office, retail, and commercial uses and the Residential 
Mixed-Use Overlay District allows residential uses as part of a mixed-use development. Therefore, the proposed 
amendment would align the existing development with a zoning designation that is compatible with the General Plan 2040 
land use designation of Mixed-Use Medium II. In addition, the proposed rezoning will eliminate the split-zoning and result 
in a single zone of C2-1/R4 throughout the entire lot. 
There is no net loss in residential capacity with this zoning map amendment and the rezoning is consistent with State law 
(Housing Crisis Act, Government Code Section 66300(b)(1)(A)).The proposed zoning map amendment also does not involve 
a decrease in the allowable building height, maximum allowable density, or FAR; nor would it increase requirements for lot 
size, setbacks, or other development requirements or standards that would lessen the intensity of housing. 
Interior Improvements
The proposed project consists of interior modifications to the existing four-story building as detailed in the table below. 
The building’s total square footage and parking spaces would remain the same.

The project will maintain its existing off-street parking layout with 112 unassigned parking spaces, of which 84 are in the 
subterranean garage and 28 are in the surface parking lot. This project is located within one-half mile of the Hayward Park 
Caltrain Station and therefore is not required to provide off-street parking spaces for residential or commercial uses, 
pursuant to Assembly Bill (AB) 2097. A full list of the required and proposed changes to the building are listed in the 
project data sheet (Attachment 7). 
Modification to Conditions of Approval
With the proposed interior modifications to remove the live-work units and replace them with independent residential 
units, two Conditions of Approval from the 1997 Council approval need to be updated to align with this change. To remove 
the live-work requirement and ensure that residential uses are maintained as part of the project to account for the 
allowed FAR and height increases that were approved with the original application, the Condition #69 would be updated as 
follows: 
69. RESIDENTIAL UN–TS - The building shall maintain a minimum of ten (10) residential units for the life of the 
project, unless modified by a City-approved entitlement, in order to remain consistent with the requirements of 
PA97-033, through which height and intensity bonuses were provided in exchange for a residential component 
and specified amenities. The residential units shall be the same sizes or larger and provide the same or greater 
number of bedrooms as the approved plans submitted on December 15, 2023. (PLANNING)
In addition, Condition #57 which sets restrictions related to the live-work units would be deleted. The proposed changes 
maintain the residential components of the approved mixed-use building and is consistent with the Senate Bill (SB) 330, as 
described above. Besides modification to the conditions for the live-work units, other departments and divisions also 
provided or updated corresponding conditions of approval based on current standards for the interior improvements. The 
conditions of approval from PA-1997-033, previously adopted by City Council on May 18, 1998, are superseded by those in 
PA-2023-033, and the full list of proposed effective conditions are included as Exhibit A under Attachment 2. With these 
updated conditions, the Site Plan and Architecture Review would be in compliance with all applicable requirements. 
Details on the findings for approval are included in Attachment 2.
PUBLIC COMMENTS:
The applicant held a formal Neighborhood Informational Meeting on September 19, 2023. The applicant’s summary of the 
neighborhood meeting is included as Attachment 8. The Planning Commission heard the item on April 23, 2024. One 
member of the public expressed concerns about ingress and egress and inquired about the scope of work relating to the 
floor plan reconfiguration. 
NEXT STEPS:
If the Council approves the project, the Zoning Map Amendment Ordinance will be brought back for adoption (second 
reading) at a subsequent meeting. The ordinance would be effective 30 days after adoption. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Section 15183 (Projects Consistent with a Community 
Plan, General Plan, or Zoning), the zoning map amendment from R3 to C2-1/R4, the interior changes to the building and 
the modifications to the conditions of approval are exempt from further environmental review because the project is 
consistent with the development density established by the General Plan policies for which an Environmental Impact 
Report (EIR) was certified on March 18, 2024. In addition, the map amendment to C2-1/R4 is consistent with the current 
General Plan land use designation for the property; no additional floor area or exterior modification to the existing 
building are proposed; there are no project specific effects which are peculiar to the project or its site; there are no project 
specific impacts which the General Plan EIR failed to analyze as significant effects; there are no potentially significant off-
site and cumulative impacts which the General Plan EIR failed to evaluate; and there is no substantial new information 
which results in more severe impacts than anticipated by the General Plan EIR.  Furthermore, any future development of

the site would be subject to a project specific environmental assessment consistent with the CEQA statutes and guidelines 
in effect at that time.
